proposals.title: The Carceral Feedback Loop: Testing the Causal Effect of Exposure to Punitive Policies on Racial Animus
proposals.motivation: A dominant model in criminology and political science posits that racial animus is a primary driver of support for punitive policies like aggressive policing and mass incarceration. However, this unidirectional causal assumption overlooks the potential for the criminal justice system itself to shape public attitudes. Drawing on Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient and framed-group-position theory, this study investigates whether exposure to punitive policies reinforces and amplifies racial resentment, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of punitiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to information about, or lived experience within, punitive criminal justice policies (e.g., stop-and-frisk, mandatory minimums) causes an increase in expressed racial animus and negative stereotyping among white Americans, particularly those with pre-existing conservative or Republican political identities.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-wave, longitudinal panel survey design with embedded survey experiments. A nationally representative sample of white Americans will be recruited via a pre-2026 online panel (e.g., Lucid, YouGov). The primary analysis will use cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) with structural equation modeling to assess the reciprocal relationships between policy support and racial animus over time. Concurrently, experimental vignettes will randomly assign participants to detailed, factual treatments about the scale and racial disparity of specific punitive policies (vs. control information), measuring immediate post-treatment changes in racial resentment scales (e.g., the Symbolic Racism scale) and social distance measures.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct, longitudinal test of the 'carceral feedback' hypothesis, challenging the foundational causal logic of the racial animus model. It will extend framed-group-position theory by demonstrating how institutional frames (policy exposure) dynamically reshape intergroup attitudes. The findings have significant implications for understanding the resilience of punitive politics and for interventions aimed at reducing racial bias in public opinion.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents a correlation between racial attitudes and policy preferences, this study innovates by rigorously testing the reversed causal pathway—that punitive policy exposure *increases* animus—a proposition implied but not directly tested in prior critical scholarship on the systemic production of racial ideology.

proposals.title: The Evolution of the Racial-Partisan Gradient: A Longitudinal Test of Framed-Group-Position Theory on Policing Attitudes in the 21st Century
proposals.motivation: Public attitudes toward the police have undergone significant transformation in the 21st century, shaped by high-profile incidents, social movements like Black Lives Matter, and deepening political polarization. Understanding how the explanatory power of key theoretical models, such as the racial-partisan gradient derived from framed-group-position theory, has changed over this period is critical for both scholarship and policy. This analysis addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by assessing whether foundational models retain their validity in a new social and political context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the explanatory power of the racial-partisan gradient model for predicting policing attitudes has increased significantly in national surveys conducted after 2010 compared to earlier periods, with political party identification becoming a stronger predictor relative to racial group identity. Furthermore, we expect the model's fit to vary systematically based on how survey questions are framed (e.g., 'law and order' vs. 'police reform').
proposals.method: We will replicate and extend Cullen's prior analysis using pooled data from national surveys (e.g., General Social Survey, American National Election Studies, Pew Research Center) from 2000-2025. We will estimate a series of hierarchical linear models and logistic regression models, with policing attitudes (support, trust, perceived bias) as the dependent variables. Key independent variables will include race, party identification, and their interaction, alongside controls for demographics and ideological frames embedded in survey questions. Model comparison will use metrics like R-squared, AIC, and cross-validated predictive accuracy to assess changes in explanatory power over time and across question frames.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal test of the framed-group-position theory in the post-2000 era, documenting how the relationship between race, politics, and policing attitudes has evolved. It will offer empirical evidence on whether criminological theories require updating for contemporary public opinion and will inform the design of future surveys and policy debates around police legitimacy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established the racial-partisan gradient, no study has systematically tested whether this model's strength and structure have changed across two decades marked by profound societal shifts. The analysis innovatively treats question framing not just as a control but as a moderator of the core theoretical relationship.

proposals.title: Testing Framed-Group-Position, Racial Resentment, and Partisan-Identity Models: A National Study of Public Support for Three-Strikes and Mandatory Minimum Sentencing Laws
proposals.motivation: Public support is a key driver of punitive sentencing policies, yet the mechanisms underlying this support remain contested. Pre-2026 research, including Cullen's work, identifies a strong racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes, but it is unclear which theoretical model best explains support for specific, impactful sentencing statutes. Understanding whether support stems from framed-group-position, racial resentment, or simple partisan identity is crucial for diagnosing the roots of mass incarceration and informing democratic discourse on criminal justice reform.
proposals.hypothesis: The Framed-Group-Position Theory will provide a superior explanatory model for support of three-strikes laws and mandatory minimums compared to models of pure racial resentment or partisan identity, as policy support is hypothesized to be a function of perceived group threat that is actively framed by political and media discourse.
proposals.method: We will field a large, national survey (N~2,500) with a diverse sample. The survey will include validated scales for the key independent variables: framed-group-position (using vignettes and group-threat items), racial resentment (modern racism scale), and partisan identity strength. Dependent variables will be multi-item measures of support for three-strikes laws and mandatory minimums. Analysis will employ hierarchical linear regression and structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the comparative predictive power and fit of the three theoretical models, controlling for demographics, crime salience, and prior victimization.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test and compare three dominant theories of punitive policy attitudes, moving beyond policing to the critical domain of sentencing. It will clarify the precise role of race, partisanship, and framing in sustaining support for controversial sentencing laws. The findings will provide empirical grounding for theoretical refinement in public opinion research and offer evidence-based insights for advocates and policymakers seeking to engage the public on sentencing reform.
proposals.novelty: While the racial-partisan link is well-established, this study innovates by pitting the novel Framed-Group-Position Theory against two established rivals in a head-to-head test focused on specific sentencing policies, rather than general attitudes toward police or punishment.

proposals.title: Beyond American Exceptionalism: A Cross-National Test of Competing Theories of Punitiveness
proposals.motivation: Criminological theories of public punitiveness, such as the racial animus, partisan sorting, and framed-group-position models, have been developed and tested almost exclusively in the U.S. context. This limits our understanding of whether these are general theories of penal attitudes or artifacts of America's unique racial history and political structure. Testing them in nations with different penal histories (e.g., less racialized or less politically polarized) is crucial for establishing theoretical generalizability and identifying the core drivers of punitive sentiment globally.
proposals.hypothesis: The explanatory power of racial animus, partisan identity, and framed-group-position theories for punitive attitudes will vary systematically across national contexts, with racial animus being less predictive in less racially stratified societies and partisan sorting being more predictive in highly polarized multiparty systems.
proposals.method: We will conduct nationally representative surveys in three strategically selected countries (e.g., Canada, Germany, and Sweden) with varying penal histories and social structures. Surveys will replicate validated U.S. measures for punitive attitudes, racial resentment, political ideology/party identification, and perceptions of group threat. We will employ multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the fit of the three competing theoretical models across country samples, comparing path coefficients and model fit indices (e.g., CFI, RMSEA) to assess contextual variation. Analyses will control for national-level factors like incarceration rates and crime statistics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct comparative test of leading U.S.-based theories of punitiveness, moving the field from a focus on American exceptionalism toward a more general, context-aware criminology of public opinion. It will clarify which social cleavages—race, party, or framed group position—are fundamental to punitive sentiment cross-nationally, informing both theoretical development and the design of global penal policy.
proposals.novelty: While comparative penal attitudes research exists, it has not directly pitted these three sophisticated, politically and racially grounded U.S. theories against each other in non-U.S. contexts. This approach moves beyond descriptive country comparisons to a theory-testing paradigm, challenging the assumed universality of models derived from the American experience.

proposals.title: Beyond Explicit Attitudes: Implicit Racial Bias, Framed-Group-Position, and Public Support for Policing Policy
proposals.motivation: Research on public attitudes toward police and criminal justice policy, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, has relied on explicit survey measures. These are vulnerable to social desirability bias, potentially obscuring the full role of racial animus in shaping policy preferences. Integrating implicit measures is crucial for a more accurate assessment of the psychological underpinnings of support for punitive versus supportive policing reforms.
proposals.hypothesis: Implicit racial bias, as measured by the IAT, will significantly predict opposition to progressive policing policies (e.g., demilitarization, community oversight) and support for punitive policies, even after controlling for explicit racial attitudes and partisan identification. This effect will be strongest among individuals whose explicit attitudes are moderate, revealing a 'hidden' racialized dimension of their policy preferences.
proposals.method: We will embed a Race-Weapons Implicit Association Test (IAT) within a national online survey (n=2,000). The IAT will follow the standard Greenwald et al. (1998) algorithm, measuring response latency associations between Black/White faces and weapons/harmless objects. This will be paired with explicit measures of racial resentment, symbolic racism, and Cullen's framed-group-position items. Dependent variables will include support for specific policies like universal second-look sentencing and defunding/refunding the police. Analysis will use hierarchical regression and latent class analysis to model the joint influence of implicit and explicit attitudes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first criminological test integrating the IAT into a framed-group-position theory framework, offering a more complete psychological account of policing attitudes. It will advance methodological rigor by confronting the 'hidden measurement crisis' regarding racial bias. Findings will inform the political discourse on policing by empirically disentangling the roles of implicit bias, explicit prejudice, and partisanship.
proposals.novelty: While the IAT is established in social psychology, its application to criminological policy attitudes is rare. Combining it with Cullen's political-sociological framed-group-position theory creates a novel multi-level model of attitude formation, moving beyond treating bias solely as an individual psychological trait to one activated by group-based political narratives.

proposals.title: The Intersection of Race, Crime, and Criminal History: A Factorial Experiment on Public Support for Police Discretion and Sentencing Reform
proposals.motivation: Public attitudes are central to democratic policing and sentencing policy, yet they are shaped by complex interactions of racial bias, perceptions of offender dangerousness, and political ideology. Pre-2026 research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, shows that support for punitive or reformist policies is not monolithic but is 'framed' by group positions. Isolating the causal weight of specific offender characteristics (e.g., race, crime type, prior record) within these frames is critical for understanding the roots of policy resistance or support, especially for reforms like 'second look' sentencing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect of an offender's race on public support for police leniency or sentencing review is not direct but is conditionally causal, moderated by the interplay of crime type (violent vs. non-violent) and criminal history, with these effects further varying by respondent partisanship and racial attitudes.
proposals.method: We will employ a 2 (Offender Race: Black/White) x 3 (Crime Type: violent felony/non-violent felony/drug possession) x 2 (Criminal History: first-time/repeat offender) between-subjects factorial vignette experiment, embedded in a national survey. Using a fully randomized design, we will present participants with a detailed scenario and measure outcomes on Likert scales for support for police officer discretion (e.g., issue a warning vs. arrest) and for a universal 'second look' sentence review. Data analysis will use OLS regression with interaction terms and Johnson-Neyman plots to identify significant moderation effects, controlling for respondent demographics, racial resentment, and political affiliation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide causal, granular evidence on how racialized perceptions of culpability and risk are constructed, directly informing the theoretical development of framed-group-position theory in criminology. It will offer practical insights for advocates and policymakers on how to message sentencing reforms (like second look policies) to diverse publics by identifying which contextual factors most trigger or mitigate racial bias in policy judgments.
proposals.novelty: While prior surveys document correlations, this experiment uniquely isolates the *causal interaction* between race, crime type, and criminal history—key dimensions in real-world discretion—and tests their effect on both frontline policing *and* back-end sentencing policy within a single design, bridging two often-separate literatures.

proposals.title: Mapping the Cognitive Architecture of Punishment: A Network Analysis of Interconnected Racial, Political, and Crime Beliefs
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, demonstrates that attitudes toward police and punishment are deeply intertwined with racial and political identities. However, pre-2026 studies often treat these attitudes as isolated variables, missing the holistic cognitive structure in which they are embedded. Understanding the interconnected belief systems that underpin public support for punitive or supportive policies is crucial for explaining policy preferences and designing effective communication strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that attitudes on race, crime, and punishment form distinct, internally coherent cognitive networks that vary systematically by racial group and political ideology, and that the centrality of racial resentment within an individual's belief network will be the strongest predictor of support for punitive criminal justice policies.
proposals.method: We will field a national survey (n=2,000) measuring a battery of established constructs: racial resentment, perceived criminal threat, trust in police, support for rehabilitative policies, and political ideology. We will construct individual-level undirected Gaussian Graphical Models (GGMs) using the `qgraph` package in R to estimate partial correlation networks, identifying which attitudes are directly connected. We will then use network comparison tests to analyze structural differences between Black and white respondents, and between Democrats and Republicans, focusing on metrics like network density, centrality (strength, betweenness), and the stability of the racial resentment node.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlational analyses to model the causal architecture of belief systems, visually and quantitatively mapping how core attitudes reinforce one another. It will provide a novel empirical test of framed-group-position theory at the cognitive level and offer a methodological advance for criminological attitude research by introducing network analysis as a tool to address the 'hidden measurement crisis' through a systems lens.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is established in clinical psychology (e.g., for psychopathology), its application to criminological and racial attitude research is novel. This approach uniquely shifts the unit of analysis from isolated variables to the system of beliefs itself, allowing us to identify which specific attitudinal connections are most constitutive of a punitive worldview.

proposals.title: Partisan Identity as a Causal Precursor: Testing the Effect of Right-Wing Political Identification on White Nationalist Beliefs in the Context of Policing and Sentencing Policy
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, often treats racial animus as a mediator explaining partisan differences in policy support. However, the causal direction between political identity and racialized beliefs remains contested. This is critical for understanding the roots of opposition to progressive criminal justice reforms (e.g., universal second look sentencing) and for designing effective interventions to build public support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, in the context of criminal legal attitudes, an individual's identification with the political right causally increases their adherence to white nationalist beliefs, rather than these beliefs merely mediating a pre-existing partisan effect.
proposals.method: We will analyze national survey data using a combination of structural equation modeling (SEM) and causal mediation analysis with inverse odds ratio weighting. To strengthen causal inference, we will employ a two-wave panel design where political identification is measured at Time 1 and white nationalist beliefs (using a validated multi-item scale) at Time 2, controlling for baseline racial attitudes. We will test alternative causal models (e.g., parallel response, reverse causation) to assess model fit and the robustness of the primary hypothesized direction.
proposals.contribution: This study will advance framed-group-position theory by explicitly modeling partisan identity as a causal driver of racialized worldviews in the legal domain. It will provide empirical evidence on the generative role of political affiliation in shaping racial animus, offering a more nuanced understanding of the attitudinal barriers to equitable criminal justice policy.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard mediation model used in much public opinion research, treating partisan identity not merely as a proxy for bundled attitudes but as an active social identity that can foster specific racial ideologies. It directly tests a core, under-examined assumption about the causal ordering of political and racial attitudes.

proposals.title: Beyond Criminal Charges: Testing a Framed-Group-Position Theory of Public Attitudes Toward Civil Liability and Impeachment
proposals.motivation: Criminological research on public attitudes has focused overwhelmingly on criminal justice outcomes like sentencing and policing, neglecting parallel domains of official accountability. In the pre-2026 context of intense political polarization and debates over institutional legitimacy (e.g., high-profile civil lawsuits against public figures, presidential impeachments), it is critical to understand if the racial-partisan divides observed in criminal justice attitudes generalize to other formal mechanisms of sanction. This study examines whether the dynamics of support for holding powerful actors accountable are consistent across legal domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core mechanisms of framed-group-position theory—whereby racial attitudes and partisan identity interact to shape perceptions of threat and deservingness—will predict public attitudes toward civil liability and impeachment in patterns analogous to those found for criminal charges, but that the effects will be moderated by the perceived political stakes and procedural formality of the sanction.
proposals.method: We will design and field a nationally representative survey experiment (n≈2,400) using pre-2026 online panels (e.g., Lucid, YouGov). The experiment will employ a 2x2x2 factorial design, varying the race (Black/White) and partisan affiliation (Democrat/Republican) of a hypothetical public official (e.g., a mayor) accused of misconduct, and the type of proceeding (criminal indictment, civil lawsuit for damages, impeachment/removal). Primary dependent variables will be support for the proceeding and perceived fairness. Analyses will use OLS regression with interaction terms and mediation analysis (PROCESS macro) to test for moderated mediation by racial resentment and partisan identity.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory beyond criminal justice, testing its generalizability as a theory of public attitudes toward formal accountability. It will provide empirical evidence on whether the deep racial-partisan schism in the U.S. is specific to criminal law or reflects a broader worldview applied to multiple governance institutions. Findings will inform debates on the politicization of law and the conditions for bipartisan support for accountability.
proposals.novelty: While framed-group-position theory has been applied to policing and sentencing, its application to civil liability and political impeachment is novel. Directly comparing attitudes across these distinct legal domains within a single theoretical and experimental framework is a significant advance over existing siloed research.

proposals.title: Testing the Stability of the Racial-Partisan Gradient: A Quasi-Experimental Analysis of Policing Attitudes Following High-Profile Legal Events
proposals.motivation: The 2020s have seen numerous high-profile legal events, such as indictments and public hearings, related to police conduct and racial justice. These events represent critical natural experiments that can test the robustness of established theoretical models of public opinion. Understanding whether the racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes is a stable feature of American political culture or is susceptible to disruption by salient institutional actions is crucial for both theory and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core relationship between racial group identification, partisan affiliation, and support for punitive policing policies, as posited by framed-group-position theory, will persist but that the strength of this gradient will be attenuated among politically attentive segments of the public following indictments or hearings that formally condemn police misconduct.
proposals.method: This study will employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal survey design. We will field two nationally representative surveys (n≈2,000 each) using online panels: a baseline survey and a follow-up survey deployed after a pre-specified, high-profile legal event (e.g., a federal indictment or congressional hearing). The analysis will use moderated OLS regression models, replicating the core specifications from Cullen's prior work. The key interaction terms will test whether the event moderates the relationship between race/partisanship and dependent variables measuring support for aggressive policing, police reform, and defunding.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct test of the stability of the racial-partisan gradient under conditions of formal legal censure, advancing framed-group-position theory beyond static correlations. It will offer empirical evidence on whether institutional accountability mechanisms can temporarily disrupt deeply entrenched political attitudes, informing debates on the efficacy of legal actions as tools for social change.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes the gradient's existence, no study has leveraged real-world legal events as exogenous shocks to test its boundaries. This moves from descriptive modeling to a dynamic test of theoretical mechanisms, a novel application of political event analysis within criminological public opinion research.

proposals.title: Beyond Partisanship: Institutional Trust as a Mediator of White Nationalism's Influence on Legal and Policing Attitudes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, establishes political identification as a powerful mediator between racial animus and policy attitudes. However, this focus may obscure other critical social-psychological pathways. Understanding the full spectrum of mechanisms, particularly trust in core legal institutions, is essential for a more complete theoretical model of how white nationalist sentiments translate into support for or opposition to specific criminal justice policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (a) white nationalist beliefs will be negatively associated with trust in federal legal institutions (e.g., the FBI, federal courts) and positively associated with trust in local police, and (b) these divergent trust orientations will significantly mediate the relationship between white nationalism and attitudes toward national versus local criminal legal policies, independent of political party identification.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from a large, national probability survey (e.g., the American National Election Studies or a custom survey) fielded pre-2026. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will specify a multiple mediation model. The exogenous variable is a latent construct of white nationalism (using established scales). The key mediators are multi-item scales measuring trust in federal legal institutions and trust in local police. The outcome variables will include support for national policies (e.g., federal sentencing reform) and local policies (e.g., expanding police powers). We will use bootstrapping (e.g., 5,000 resamples) in software like Mplus or the lavaan package in R to test the indirect effects, controlling for party ID, ideology, and demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will advance framed-group-position theory by delineating how institutional trust channels racialized group positioning into specific policy preferences. It will provide a more nuanced account of legal attitudes than partisanship alone, identifying distinct trust-based pathways for national versus local policy domains. The findings will inform interventions aimed at building institutional legitimacy across racial and political divides.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links racial attitudes to trust and partisanship separately, this proposal uniquely tests institutional trust as a parallel mediator to party ID, hypothesizing a *differential* trust pattern (federal vs. local) that directly maps onto policy level (national vs. local), a theoretically specified mechanism not yet fully examined in Cullen's prior work on the racial-partisan gradient.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Racialized Legal Attitudes: A Longitudinal Test of Framed-Group-Position Theory on White Nationalism, Partisanship, and Attitude Stability
proposals.motivation: The stability of public attitudes toward legal institutions is a cornerstone of democratic legitimacy, yet recent scholarship highlights a deepening racial-partisan divide in policing and criminal justice opinions. The rise of white nationalist and ethno-nationalist ideologies in mainstream political discourse (pre-2026) presents a critical but understudied factor that may harden racialized attitudes and undermine the responsiveness of legal systems to reform. Understanding how these ideologies interact with partisanship to shape attitude stability over time is essential for diagnosing the roots of political polarization on legal policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adherence to white nationalist beliefs will predict greater stability in racially charged legal attitudes (e.g., support for aggressive policing, opposition to sentencing reform) over time, and that this effect will be most pronounced among White respondents and will moderate the well-documented influence of political partisanship on attitude formation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-wave national panel survey of American adults over 24 months, oversampling White respondents. The survey will include validated scales measuring white nationalist beliefs (e.g., adapted from Jardina's 'White Identity' measures), partisan identification, and a battery of legal attitude items on policing, sentencing, and procedural justice. We will employ latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) and cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) to analyze intra-individual change and the reciprocal relationships between ideology, partisanship, and attitudes. Analyses will control for key demographics and media consumption patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, nationally representative test of how white nationalist ideology shapes the durability of legal attitudes, directly extending Cullen's framed-group-position theory. It will empirically clarify whether racialized ideologies act as a foundational schema that anchors attitudes beyond partisan signaling, offering a more nuanced understanding of the mechanisms driving polarization in criminal justice policy preferences.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes a racial-partisan gradient in attitudes at a single point in time, this project innovates by examining the dynamic, temporal role of a specific ideological construct—white nationalism—in cementing attitude stability, moving beyond descriptive correlations to model causal processes of attitude entrenchment.

proposals.title: Implicit Partisan-Racial Bias and Support for Policing Policy: A Framed-Group-Position Analysis
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Cullen and colleagues demonstrates that explicit policing attitudes are structured by a racial-partisan gradient, but reliance on self-report surveys risks social desirability bias and misses unconscious associations. Given the national debate over police reform and racial justice, understanding the implicit, automatic biases that may undergird public opinion and policy preferences is critical. This gap represents a hidden measurement crisis in criminological public opinion research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implicit associations linking racial groups with partisan identities (e.g., faster reaction times pairing 'Black' with 'Democrat' and 'White' with 'Republican') will significantly predict opposition to progressive policing reforms, even after controlling for explicit political and racial attitudes. This effect will be strongest among individuals who explicitly deny racial bias.
proposals.method: We will administer a custom Implicit Association Test (IAT) to a nationally representative sample of white Americans (n=1,500). The IAT will measure the strength of association between racial group stimuli (e.g., Black/White faces) and partisan identity labels (Democrat/Republican). Following the IAT, participants will complete Cullen's established survey battery measuring explicit framed-group-position attitudes, political ideology, and support for specific policies (e.g., universal second look sentencing, demilitarization). Data analysis will use hierarchical regression and structural equation modeling to test the unique predictive power of implicit partisan-racial bias on policy support.
proposals.contribution: This study will be the first to integrate implicit social cognition measures directly into the framed-group-position theory of policing attitudes, moving beyond explicit self-reports. It will provide empirical evidence for how unconscious partisan-racial schemas shape criminal justice policy preferences, offering a more complete model of public opinion. The findings will inform strategies for communicating reform proposals to a polarized public.
proposals.novelty: While IATs are common in social psychology, their application to criminological policy attitudes is rare. Combining the IAT with Cullen's nuanced framed-group-position framework to dissect the implicit roots of the racial-partisan gradient is a novel theoretical and methodological synthesis.

proposals.title: Networked Beliefs: Mapping the Structure Connecting White Nationalism, Partisan Identity, and Legal Attitudes in the American Public
proposals.motivation: In the mid-2020s, American public opinion on policing and criminal justice is deeply polarized along racial and partisan lines, as Cullen's prior work shows. Understanding how extremist ideologies like white nationalism are integrated into broader political belief systems is crucial, as these networks may drive opposition to equitable legal reforms and shape policy debates. This project addresses a gap in criminological measurement by examining the structural connections between these core attitudes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that white nationalist beliefs are not isolated but form a central, densely connected node in a belief network for a subset of Americans, strongly linked to partisan identity and serving as a key predictor of opposition to progressive legal policies (e.g., sentencing reform, police accountability).
proposals.method: We will collect a large, national survey sample measuring white nationalism (using validated scales), partisan identity, and attitudes toward policing and sentencing policies. We will employ Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) network analysis to estimate partial correlation networks, identifying the unique conditional associations between beliefs. We will use the R package `qgraph` for network estimation and `bootnet` for stability analysis, comparing network structure and centrality metrics (e.g., strength, betweenness) across racial and partisan subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of how white nationalism is structurally embedded within political and legal belief systems, advancing Cullen's 'Framed-Group-Position Theory.' It will offer a novel methodological approach to the 'hidden measurement crisis' by modeling attitudes as interdependent networks rather than isolated variables, informing more effective public messaging and policy design.
proposals.novelty: While prior research correlates these attitudes, network analysis uniquely reveals the *architecture* of belief systems, identifying which connections are direct and which are mediated. This moves beyond regression to show how white nationalism may act as a hub that organizes other political and legal views.

proposals.title: Localized Legitimacy: Applying a Framed-Group-Position Theory to Public Attitudes on Criminal Charges Against Local Officials
proposals.motivation: Public trust in government is critically shaped by perceptions of local officials' integrity. Pre-2026 research on political scandals and policing attitudes often focuses on national figures or abstract policy, neglecting how partisanship and racial group position shape reactions to concrete criminal allegations against proximate, local actors. This gap limits understanding of how legal accountability is socially constructed in communities.
proposals.hypothesis: Public support for criminal charges against a local official will be structured by a racial-partisan gradient, where attitudes are a function of the interaction between the respondent's partisan identity, the official's partisan identity, and the racial context of the locality, as predicted by framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, vignette-based survey experiment. A nationally representative sample (n≈3000) will be randomly assigned vignettes manipulating the local official's partisanship (Democrat/Republican), the nature of the alleged crime (corruption vs. assault), and the racial demographics of the hypothetical municipality (majority-White, majority-Black, majority-Hispanic). Dependent variables will measure support for prosecution and perceived legitimacy of the charges. Analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to account for individual and contextual factors, testing for the predicted three-way interaction between respondent party ID, official party ID, and locale racial context.
proposals.contribution: This study extends Cullen's framed-group-position theory from policing to the domain of political corruption and local governance, testing its generalizability. It will produce novel insights into how racial and partisan identities jointly condition perceptions of legal accountability for elites, informing debates on the legitimacy of local justice systems.
proposals.novelty: While group-position theory has been applied to policing, its application to criminal charges against local elected officials—where partisan and racial frames are highly salient but understudied in tandem—is novel. The design explicitly tests how local racial context moderates partisan motivated reasoning in scandals.

proposals.title: The Punitive Turn? Testing Framed-Group-Position Theory on Support for Criminal Charges in High-Profile Cases
proposals.motivation: Criminological research on public opinion has focused heavily on support for leniency and decarceration, leaving a gap in understanding the drivers of punitive attitudes, especially in the post-2020 era of heightened racial justice activism and political polarization. This study addresses the inverse: what factors predict strong public support for filing criminal charges, particularly in cases involving police or racial conflict? Understanding this is crucial for a complete picture of the political and social dynamics shaping criminal justice policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Drawing on Cullen's framed-group-position theory, we hypothesize that support for criminal charges is not monolithic but is structured along a racial-partisan gradient, where liberal ideology and anti-racist attitudes are significant positive predictors of support for charges against police or public officials, while conservative ideology predicts support for charges against protesters or activists.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N=2,500) of U.S. adults. The core method is a factorial vignette design, manipulating the race/partisanship of the alleged offender (e.g., police officer vs. civilian protester) and the context of the alleged crime. Dependent variables will be Likert-scale measures of support for filing charges. Analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to account for vignette nesting and OLS regression with interaction terms to test the framed-group-position model, controlling for demographics, prior victimization, and media consumption.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct empirical test of whether the racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes extends to support for initiating charges, thereby refining framed-group-position theory. It will contribute to the literature on public opinion and legal cynicism by mapping the conditions under which the public demands state punitive action, offering insights for policymakers and court officials navigating high-profile cases.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant research focus on support for leniency and directly applies Cullen's theoretical framework to the understudied domain of charge-filing support, moving beyond attitudes toward police to attitudes toward prosecution. It treats political ideology and racial attitudes as potential drivers of *pro*-prosecution sentiment, not just resistance to it.

proposals.title: Mapping Contextual Validity: An Adaptive Procedural Justice Scale for Adjudicating the Ecological Fit of Policing and Justice Measures
proposals.motivation: Criminological measurement faces a hidden crisis, where static scales may not capture how constructs like procedural justice are perceived across diverse racial and political groups. This is critical for policing research, as public attitudes and policy support are shaped by these perceptions. Without understanding the contextual boundaries of our measures, we risk misdiagnosing public sentiment and the legitimacy of institutions.
proposals.hypothesis: The validity and reliability of established static measures of procedural justice and related constructs are not fixed but vary systematically based on contextual factors such as community racial composition, pre-existing trust in police, and national political climate.
proposals.method: We will first develop a Dynamic Procedural Justice Scale (DPJS) using a machine learning framework (e.g., Bayesian Additive Regression Trees or a multi-level IRT model) where item parameters are conditioned on contextual moderators (e.g., census data, trust indices, institutional type). This DPJS will serve as a context-sensitive benchmark. We will then conduct a large-scale meta-analytic validation study, applying the DPJS to adjudicate a corpus of pre-2026 static measures (e.g., from the National Police Attitudes Survey, General Social Survey) across simulated and real-world subsamples defined by the moderators, generating a 'validity map'.
proposals.contribution: This provides a novel meta-validation methodology that moves beyond declaring measures universally good/bad to specifying the ecological niches where they perform well or poorly. It directly addresses the measurement crisis by offering a dynamic, evidence-based framework for selecting and interpreting measures in specific research and policy contexts, particularly those involving racial-partisan gradients in attitudes.
proposals.novelty: While procedural justice scales and validation studies exist, the creation of an adaptive scale as a benchmarking tool to contextually adjudicate the fitness of other measures represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard multi-group invariance testing.

proposals.title: Beyond Paradox: Theorizing Fear and Support for Police as a Rational Coping Strategy in High-Crime, Under-Policed Communities
proposals.motivation: Criminological and public opinion research often frames fear of crime and support for police as paradoxical, especially among Black Americans, given evidence of over-policing and police misconduct. This paradox is a central tension in Cullen's recent work on the racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes. However, this framing may obscure a more complex reality where, in contexts of both high crime and perceived police neglect, fear and support are not contradictory but are rational, intertwined responses to a dual threat of victimization and institutional abandonment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in high-crime, under-policed environments, fear of crime and support for police are positively correlated, and that this relationship is mediated by a perceived need for protective efficacy and moderated by racial group consciousness and political ideology, as predicted by a framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from the 2024 American National Election Studies (ANES) Time Series Survey and geocode it with tract-level Uniform Crime Report (UCR) data. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a moderated mediation model where fear of crime predicts support for police via perceived protective need. Multi-group analysis will compare Black and white respondents, and we will test interactions with partisanship and measures of linked fate to assess the framed-group-position framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will advance framed-group-position theory by specifying a mechanism—rational coping—that explains the co-occurrence of fear and support. It will challenge a core assumption in the literature and provide a more nuanced, context-dependent model of public attitudes toward policing, particularly for marginalized communities. The findings will inform policy debates on police legitimacy and community safety by highlighting the conditional nature of support.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reconceptualizing the fear-support link not as a paradox to be resolved but as a rational, context-driven strategy, directly challenging a long-standing interpretive frame in criminological public opinion research. It integrates ecological context (crime rates, policing levels) with social-psychological group-position processes in a novel way.

proposals.title: Beyond Monoliths: A Latent Class Analysis of Black American Subgroup Attitudes Toward Policing and Criminal Justice Policy
proposals.motivation: Criminological research, including Cullen's recent work on the racial-partisan gradient, often treats Black American opinion as a monolithic bloc, potentially obscuring critical intra-group variation. This assumption limits theoretical precision and policy relevance, as it fails to account for how class, geography, and lived experience shape perspectives on police legitimacy and reform. Addressing this gap is essential for developing more nuanced, framed-group-position theories and for crafting policies that resonate with diverse constituencies within Black communities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Black American attitudes toward policing and criminal justice reform are not uniform but are structured into distinct latent classes defined by socioeconomic status, residential context (urban/suburban/rural), and personal/vicarious victimization history. These subgroups will demonstrate systematically different levels of support for policies ranging from procedural reform to defunding.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing national survey data (e.g., the General Social Survey, American National Election Studies) from 2010-2025, oversampling Black respondents. Using Latent Class Analysis (LCA), we will identify unobserved subgroups based on indicators of class (income, education), geography (census region, metro status), and victimization. We will then employ multinomial logistic regression to model class membership and use regression analyses within classes to predict policy attitudes (e.g., support for universal second look sentencing, police funding reallocation).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, empirical typology of Black American opinion subgroups on criminal justice issues, directly testing the monolithic assumption. It will refine framed-group-position theory by specifying the conditional factors that shape in-group positioning. The findings will offer policymakers a more granular map of public sentiment to better target and communicate reform initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents Black-white opinion gaps, few studies use person-centered methods like LCA to model heterogeneity *within* the Black population based on a confluence of structural and experiential factors. This moves beyond simple demographic controls to identify coherent, empirically derived typologies of belief.

proposals.title: The Paradox of Police: A Qualitative Investigation of Coexisting Fear and Support in American Public Opinion
proposals.motivation: Despite high-profile incidents of police violence and widespread calls for reform, national surveys (e.g., Gallup, GSS) consistently show robust aggregate public support for police in the United States. This apparent contradiction—coexisting fear and support—is underexplored, particularly regarding the psychological and social mechanisms that sustain support. Understanding these mechanisms is critical for advancing theories of institutional legitimacy and for informing effective, publicly-accepted policing policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robust police support is maintained through a complex of cognitive frames and group-position rationalizations, where abstract support for the institution as a necessary social function coexists with specific fears of its potential for discriminatory or harmful application, particularly along racial and partisan lines.
proposals.method: We will conduct 60-80 in-depth, semi-structured interviews with a purposively sampled, diverse cohort of U.S. residents, stratified by race (Black, White), political affiliation (Republican, Democrat, Independent), and urban/suburban/rural location. Interviews will use vignettes and open-ended questions derived from Cullen's 'framed-group-position' theory to probe perceptions of police roles, personal/community experiences, and reactions to scenarios of police conduct. Data will be analyzed using a multi-stage qualitative approach: initial open coding in NVivo, followed by focused coding to identify thematic patterns, and finally axial coding to construct a theoretical model explaining the relationship between fear and support.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, mechanism-rich theoretical model to explain the paradox of police legitimacy in a polarized era. It will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory from quantitative survey research into the qualitative realm, offering deeper insight into the lived experiences and reasoning behind public attitudes. The findings will inform more targeted and communicatively effective police reform and public engagement strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents the racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes, this study uniquely employs qualitative methods to unpack the *subjective reasoning processes* that allow fear and support to coexist within individuals and groups, moving beyond documenting *what* people think to explain *why* and *how* they hold these seemingly contradictory views.

proposals.title: Perceived Legitimacy and Professional Practice: A Framed-Group-Position Analysis of Police Officers' Community Perceptions and Reform Openness
proposals.motivation: Extensive pre-2026 research, including Cullen's work, examines public attitudes toward police, revealing a stark racial-partisan gradient. However, the inverse relationship—how officers perceive community support and legitimacy—remains a critical but understudied driver of street-level behavior and institutional reform resistance. Understanding this dynamic is essential for designing effective, sustainable police reforms that address the core relational fractures highlighted in national discourse post-2020.
proposals.hypothesis: Police officers' perceptions of community support (varying by racial/geographic context) significantly predict their self-reported procedural justice behaviors and openness to key policy reforms, with effects moderated by officers' political ideology and framed-group-position perceptions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, stratified national survey of sworn law enforcement officers (N≈2,500), employing a factorial vignette design to experimentally manipulate community demographic and behavioral cues. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a latent variable model where perceived community legitimacy mediates the relationship between experimental frames and outcome measures. Analyses will specifically model interaction effects between officer race, political affiliation, and assigned vignette conditions using moderated mediation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This study directly addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by systematically measuring the officer-side perceptual landscape. It extends Cullen's framed-group-position theory into the domain of professional subcultures, providing a symmetrical theoretical framework for police-community relations. Findings will offer evidence-based guidance for interventions aimed at improving police-community reciprocity and reducing resistance to reform.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the dominant analytical lens from public attitudes *toward* police to police attitudes *toward* the public, applying a group-position theoretical framework—typically used for public opinion—to the occupational worldview of police officers themselves.

proposals.title: Priming the Public: A Causal Test of Elite Discourse on Racialized Attributions for Crime and Policing
proposals.motivation: The 'Racial-Partisan Gradient' in policing attitudes demonstrates a deep public divide, but the causal role of elite political and media discourse in shaping these attributions remains unclear. Understanding whether elite frames drive public opinion on racialized issues like crime and police reform is critical for diagnosing polarization and informing democratic accountability, yet existing research is largely correlational.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to partisan-elite frames (e.g., framing crime as a 'law and order' vs. 'systemic injustice' issue) causally precedes and shapes the racialized attributions the public makes for crime trends and police performance, independent of baseline partisanship and racial attitudes.
proposals.method: We will implement a longitudinal cohort design using a nationally representative panel (e.g., NORC's AmeriSpeak) surveyed at three waves over 18 months. Exposure to elite discourse will be measured via validated media diet tracking and computational text analysis (using LDA topic modeling and dictionary-based framing detection on linked news consumption data). We will treat salient, real-world events (e.g., a high-profile police use-of-force incident or a major sentencing reform proposal) as natural experiments, analyzing changes in attribution scales before and after the event using fixed-effects regression models with lagged exposure variables to establish temporal precedence and control for key confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, quasi-experimental evidence for elite discourse as a causal driver of public attributions on racialized criminal justice issues. It will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by testing its causal mechanisms and offer a methodological template for moving beyond correlational findings in public opinion research.
proposals.novelty: It adapts epidemiological causal inference methods (cohort designs, natural experiments) to a core political communication question, directly testing the direction of influence between elite discourse and public opinion on a highly salient, racialized policy domain.

proposals.title: Elite Discourse and Public Attributions: An Experimental Test of Framed-Group-Position Theory in the Context of Policing and Racial Justice
proposals.motivation: Research on public attitudes toward crime and policing consistently shows a racial-partisan gradient, but the causal direction remains ambiguous. It is unclear whether elite political discourse primarily reflects or actively shapes public attributions for crime and support for punitive policies. Understanding this dynamic is critical for explaining the persistence of racialized policy preferences and the potential for meaningful reform.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to elite political frames that attribute crime to individual failings versus systemic racism will causally shift public attributions and policy preferences, with the effect moderated by racial group identification and partisan affiliation, as predicted by framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N~3,000) with a 2x2 factorial design, manipulating elite source (partisan cue) and attributional frame (individual vs. systemic). The dependent variables will be causal attributions for crime and support for specific policing policies (e.g., defunding, procedural justice reforms). Analysis will use OLS regression with interaction terms to test moderation by racial identity and partisanship, controlling for demographics and prior attitudes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct causal test of a core mechanism in framed-group-position theory, moving beyond correlational evidence. It will clarify the role of elite discourse in shaping the racial-partisan divide in criminal justice attitudes, offering insights for scholars and advocates seeking to communicate effectively about reform.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents the correlation between partisanship and attributions, this study experimentally isolates the causal effect of elite frames, testing whether they can reshape—not just reflect—the foundational group-position perceptions that undergird public opinion on racialized issues like policing.

proposals.title: Tracking the Diffusion of Blame: A Networked Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Attributional Shifts in Police Legitimacy After High-Profile Events
proposals.motivation: Public attributions for police misconduct—whether blaming 'bad apples' or systemic racism—are central to political polarization and support for reform, yet are measured statically. The post-Ferguson era and calls for defunding reveal a volatile, event-driven opinion climate. Understanding the dynamic, networked process of how these attributions form and spread is critical for policy and democratic accountability.
proposals.hypothesis: Attributional shifts following salient events will diffuse through social and media networks in predictable patterns, with velocity and final opinion crystallization moderated by pre-existing racial and partisan identities (the racial-partisan gradient) and the framing of information sources.
proposals.method: We will recruit a nationally diverse panel (n=1,500) for a 12-month longitudinal study using a smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) app. Participants will provide brief reports on police attribution beliefs multiple times weekly and after pre-identified trigger events. Ego-network data on primary information sources (e.g., specific news outlets, social media feeds) and discussion partners will be collected. We will apply stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) via the RSiena package to analyze the co-evolution of networks and attitudes, and use survival analysis to model the velocity of attribution change.
proposals.contribution: This moves beyond cross-sectional snapshots to provide a dynamic, mechanistic model of opinion formation on policing. It will identify key influencers and pathways in the attribution ecosystem, offering evidence for how policy communications might effectively intervene. It directly addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by capturing opinion as a process, not a stable trait.
proposals.novelty: While EMA and network analysis are established separately, their integration to model the real-time diffusion of political attributions is novel in criminology. This adapts epidemiological models of behavioral contagion to the study of belief formation, a significant methodological advance for public opinion research.

proposals.title: Narrative Legitimacy: An Experimental Test of Media Framing Effects on Public Trust in Criminological Measures
proposals.motivation: Public trust in criminological science is essential for evidence-based policy, yet it is often filtered through partisan and racialized media narratives. Pre-2026 research by Cullen and others demonstrates a stark racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes, suggesting that frames, not just facts, shape public opinion. This project addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by investigating whether media narratives can procedurally validate or invalidate scientific metrics themselves, thereby influencing support for policies like sentencing reform.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to excusatory (vs. individual-responsibility) media frames will not only shift causal attributions for crime but will also systematically lower perceptions of the procedural justice and legitimacy of the criminological scales presented within the narrative, even when the underlying data is identical.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 2x2 between-subjects online survey experiment (N~1,200) using a national sample. Participants will be randomly assigned to read a news article about crime trends that varies the narrative frame (excusatory/systemic vs. individual-responsibility) while presenting identical data from established scales (e.g., items from the Procedural Justice in Science scale or validated criminological risk indices). Primary dependent variables will be causal attributions and a modified procedural justice scale assessing the perceived fairness, neutrality, and trustworthiness of the research measures described. Analyses will use OLS regression with interaction terms to test for framing effects moderated by racial and political identity.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly bridge framing theory with the science of science communication, showing how narratives bias evaluations of methodological rigor. It will provide empirical evidence for how 'procedural validation' via media can exacerbate the racial-partisan divide in trust in social science. The findings will offer practical insights for researchers and journalists on presenting data without undermining its perceived legitimacy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines how frames shape policy attitudes, this study innovates by testing whether frames alter the perceived procedural justice of the scientific measures *carrying* the data—a second-order legitimizing effect not previously isolated. It fuses Cullen's work on racial-partisan gradients and measurement crises with experimental media effects research.

proposals.title: Inoculating Against Backlash: A Longitudinal Test of a Dissonance-Based Intervention for Durable Attitude Change on Policing
proposals.motivation: Deeply polarized and racialized attitudes toward policing in America, as documented by the racial-partisan gradient, undermine public support for equitable policy reform. While single-shot interventions can shift attitudes temporarily, they often fail to withstand real-world social and political counter-persuasion. This creates a critical gap in translating experimental findings into sustainable, real-world attitude change that can support long-term policy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-component intervention that sequentially induces cognitive dissonance, provides inoculation against counter-arguments, and reinforces new attitudes through values-affirmation and public commitment will produce more stable, resistant, and durable changes in policing attributions and policy support than a dissonance intervention alone.
proposals.method: We will recruit a national sample of white Americans, stratified by political affiliation, for a 6-month longitudinal experiment. The treatment arm will first complete a written counter-attitudinal advocacy task (dissonance induction) regarding a police use-of-force incident with racial disparities. This is immediately followed by three weekly 'inoculation' sessions where participants generate and refute common counter-arguments from their own political ingroup. Periodic values-affirmation exercises and small, escalating public commitments (e.g., signing a petition, sharing a non-public statement) are integrated. Control arms will receive the dissonance task only or a placebo. We will measure attitudes (e.g., attributions, support for procedural justice reforms), resistance to persuasive attacks, and behavioral intentions at multiple waves.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first rigorous tests of a theory-driven, multi-stage protocol for creating durable attitude change in a criminological context. It will advance framed-group-position theory by testing mechanisms for reducing defensive polarization. Practically, it will yield evidence-based strategies for designing public engagement campaigns that sustain support for policing reform.
proposals.novelty: The research moves beyond testing isolated psychological principles to engineering and testing a sustained behavioral intervention protocol for criminological attitudes. It uniquely combines dissonance, inoculation, and commitment techniques within a longitudinal design to directly tackle the problem of attitude decay and social backlash.

proposals.title: Inoculating Against Polarization: A Framed-Group-Position Test of Attitudinal Immunization on Police Reform
proposals.motivation: The racial-partisan gradient in policing attitudes, documented in Cullen's recent work, represents a profound threat to evidence-based policy and public safety. This polarization, exacerbated by misinformation and identity-protective cognition, undermines democratic discourse and the legitimacy of law enforcement. Adapting a proven public health framework offers a novel pathway to mitigate this entrenched ideological divide.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cognitive inoculation intervention, which exposes participants to a weakened counter-attitudinal argument about policing (e.g., a poorly reasoned 'defund' or 'unqualified support' statement) and guides them to refute it, will reduce subsequent susceptibility to stronger polarizing messages and decrease expressed attitudinal extremity on police reform.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave, online survey experiment with a national sample of U.S. adults, stratified by race and partisanship. Using a 2x2 factorial design, participants will be randomly assigned to an inoculation condition (weak counter-attitudinal message + refutation) or a control, targeting either a liberal or conservative polarizing myth. Pre- and post-intervention measures will adapt Cullen's established scales on police support, legitimacy, and reform. The primary analysis will use OLS regression with interaction terms to test for differential effects across the racial-partisan groups defined by framed-group-position theory.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of attitudinal inoculation theory applied to criminological polarization, directly extending Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient. It will generate actionable knowledge on a psychological intervention to foster more critical, less identity-driven engagement with policing debates. The findings will inform communication strategies for scholars and practitioners seeking to bridge ideological divides on criminal justice policy.
proposals.novelty: While inoculation theory is established in communication science, its application to the specific, identity-laden domain of policing attitudes—guided by framed-group-position theory—is novel. The approach shifts focus from persuading individuals to change their views to strengthening their cognitive resistance to polarizing rhetoric, a distinct strategy in criminological attitude research.

proposals.title: Dynamic Pathways to Common Ground: An Adaptive Cognitive Dissonance Framework for Reducing Racial-Partisan Polarization on Policing
proposals.motivation: Deepening racial-partisan polarization on policing, as documented in Cullen's recent work, undermines public support for evidence-based reform and erodes trust in institutions. Traditional static interventions and measurements fail to capture the dynamic, non-linear process of attitude change, limiting our ability to design effective depolarization strategies. This research addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by developing a responsive experimental paradigm to model real-time shifts in attribution and resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Adaptive cognitive dissonance interventions, where the intensity and content of counter-attitudinal prompts are dynamically tuned to a participant's real-time measured state, will identify personalized inflection points that lead to more substantial and durable reductions in polarized attitudes toward policing than static, one-size-fits-all approaches.
proposals.method: We will design an online experiment using a dynamic assessment architecture. Participants' baseline attitudes on policing and race will be measured via validated scales. During the counter-attitudinal essay task, we will implement frequent, brief 'state' measurements (e.g., single-item sliders for discomfort, agreement, and racial attribution) using a computer adaptive testing (CAT) logic to estimate a latent 'dissonance-resistance' score. A rule-based algorithm (e.g., if resistance is low, increase argument complexity; if high, simplify and affirm self-integrity) will modify subsequent essay prompts in real-time. Post-intervention, we will measure attitude change using both traditional scales and the new dynamic trajectory data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel methodological framework for measuring polarization as a dynamic process, not a static trait. It will generate empirical evidence on the 'tipping points' and personalized pathways in attitude change regarding policing, offering practical insights for crafting more effective communication and policy narratives. It directly extends Cullen's framed-group-position theory by testing its micro-level, cognitive mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in synthesizing cognitive dissonance theory with adaptive measurement technology to create a closed-loop, responsive experiment, moving beyond pre-post designs to model the *process* of depolarization in real-time, which is absent from prior work on policing attitudes.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Drivers of Public Judgment: A Conjoint Analysis of Perceived Police Misconduct Severity
proposals.motivation: Public trust in police is critical for democratic legitimacy and effective crime control, yet it is fractured, particularly along racial and partisan lines. Existing survey measures of attitudes toward police misconduct often rely on broad, single-item questions that fail to isolate the causal influence of specific incident attributes (e.g., officer intent, harm caused, suspect race) on public judgment, creating a measurement crisis for understanding the foundations of support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the public's judgment of misconduct severity is driven by a weighted, non-linear function of incident attributes, where the part-worth utility of an attribute like suspect race or officer partisanship is conditional on other contextual factors, consistent with a framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will implement a fully randomized, choice-based conjoint experiment embedded in a national survey. Respondents will be presented with paired profiles of police misconduct vignettes where attributes (e.g., officer's stated intent, severity of outcome, race of citizen, evidence of racial slur, departmental discipline) are independently varied across multiple levels. Using hierarchical Bayesian estimation via the `cjoint` package in R, we will calculate the average marginal component effect (AMCE) for each attribute to quantify its causal impact on the choice of which incident is more severe, and employ interaction models to test for conditional effects based on respondent race and partisanship.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first granular, causal map of the relative weights the American public assigns to different facets of police misconduct, moving beyond correlational findings. It will directly inform the racial-partisan gradient theory by quantifying how incident characteristics frame group positions, and offer a rigorous methodological template for measuring complex public judgments in criminology and political science.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is standard in marketing, its application to deconstruct perceptions of institutional misconduct is novel in criminology. This approach moves past simplistic 'support' measures to model the cognitive calculus of judgment, directly addressing the hidden measurement crisis identified in prior work.

proposals.title: Decomposing Public Scrutiny: A Conjoint Analysis of Causal Attributions for Police Misconduct
proposals.motivation: Public trust in police is a cornerstone of democratic legitimacy and effective crime control, yet it is fractured, particularly along racial and partisan lines. Understanding how the public judges police misconduct is critical for policy, but existing survey research struggles to isolate the causal weight of specific incident characteristics (e.g., officer intent, victim demographics, departmental response) from correlated factors. This limits our ability to predict reactions to real-world events and design effective accountability mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the public's judgment of police misconduct severity and appropriate sanction is causally driven more by perceptions of officer intent (e.g., negligence vs. malice) and departmental transparency than by victim race, but that the influence of these factors is moderated by respondent race and partisan identity, consistent with a framed-group-position perspective.
proposals.method: We will employ a fully randomized conjoint experiment embedded in a nationally representative survey (n≈3,000). Respondents will evaluate multiple paired profiles of hypothetical police misconduct incidents. Profiles will vary on key attributes identified from pre-2026 literature: officer's stated intent, victim race/age, presence of body-worn camera footage, departmental disciplinary history, and initial official statement. Using hierarchical Bayesian logistic regression models, we will estimate the average marginal component effect (AMCE) for each attribute on outcomes of perceived severity and support for officer termination. We will test for moderation by respondent race and partisanship via interaction terms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal map of how specific incident features drive public opinion on police accountability, moving beyond correlational findings. It will empirically test and refine framed-group-position theory by identifying which contextual frames most powerfully activate racial-partisan divides. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for communicators and policymakers seeking to bridge divides or promote accountability.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is established in political science, its application to decompose the cognitive heuristics behind policing judgments is novel in criminology. This directly addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by isolating causal weights of complex, real-world incident characteristics in public evaluation.

proposals.title: Scandal as Catalyst or Coincidence? A Matched-City Comparative Analysis of Police Reform Trajectories
proposals.motivation: High-profile police misconduct scandals, such as those in Ferguson and Baltimore, are widely assumed to be primary drivers of police reform. However, this assumption conflates scandal-driven change with broader secular trends in policing policy and oversight, limiting our understanding of effective reform pathways. A systematic, comparative analysis is needed to isolate the specific institutional effects of scandal from other factors shaping police governance.
proposals.hypothesis: Cities experiencing a recent, high-profile police scandal will demonstrate a significantly greater volume and pace of formal policy reforms and oversight activity compared to demographically and politically matched cities without such a scandal, even when controlling for national political discourse and pre-existing reform movements.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative case study design with propensity score matching to select 3-4 city pairs (one with a major scandal 2015-2023, one without). Data collection will involve a structured content analysis of city council minutes, police department policy manuals, and civilian oversight board reports from 2020-2024 to code reform outcomes. This will be supplemented by secondary analysis of existing officer survey datasets (e.g., the Police Executive Research Forum surveys) and systematic tracking of local media coverage to measure political and public pressure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, controlled comparison of police reform in scandal versus non-scandal contexts, moving beyond single-case outlier studies. It will directly test the framed-group-position theory in a comparative institutional setting, clarifying how racial-partisan gradients in public attitudes translate into concrete policy changes under different catalytic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines public attitudes or single scandal cases, this design's matched-city comparison directly isolates the scandal variable, addressing the 'hidden measurement crisis' by creating a counterfactual to distinguish scandal-specific effects from background trends.

proposals.title: The Louisville Laboratory: Testing Competing Theories of Police Legitimacy and Racial Division in the Aftermath of the Breonna Taylor Case
proposals.motivation: Criminology faces a hidden measurement crisis, where inconsistent findings on public trust in police obscure whether the problem is methodological or theoretical. This ambiguity impedes effective policy reform, particularly regarding profound racial and partisan divides in attitudes. A high-profile case of police misconduct provides a critical, bounded context to isolate the source of these inconsistencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when applied to the same empirical context, competing theoretical frameworks (e.g., procedural justice, institutional anomie, framed-group-position) will yield divergent explanatory power not primarily due to measurement artifact, but due to genuine theoretical incompatibility in explaining racialized attitudes and institutional legitimacy.
