proposals.method: We conduct a multi-method study in Louisville, KY, post-Breonna Taylor. We field a stratified, citywide survey (N=~1500) embedding multiple validated scales for each theoretical construct (e.g., Tyler's procedural justice items, Messner & Rosenfeld's institutional anomie items, and group-position items). We will analyze these using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test competing path models. This will be triangulated with administrative data on policing patterns and in-depth interviews with community stakeholders and police personnel.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly address the measurement crisis by diagnosing whether inconsistent literature findings are methodological or theoretical. It will advance framed-group-position theory by testing it against established paradigms in a real-world crucible. The results will provide concrete evidence for whether police reform requires cultural, institutional, or relational interventions.
proposals.novelty: The design's novelty lies in using a single high-profile case as a controlled 'living laboratory' to hold context constant, thereby isolating theoretical versus measurement variance—a direct empirical test of the field's foundational crisis that moves beyond methodological critique.

proposals.title: The Backfire Effect of Innocence: How Belief in Executed Innocents Hardens Support for Capital Punishment Among Retributive and Partisan Subgroups
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, shows that attitudes toward criminal justice policies are deeply polarized. The conventional wisdom is that evidence of wrongful executions erodes public support for the death penalty. However, motivated reasoning and group-position theories suggest this information could paradoxically strengthen support among subgroups whose identity or worldview is threatened by it, potentially explaining the stability of death penalty support despite high-profile exonerations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to information about executed innocents will increase support for capital punishment among individuals with strong retributive beliefs and among White Republicans, for whom the information challenges core beliefs about system legitimacy and in-group status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N~2,500) with a 2x2 factorial design manipulating exposure to statistical versus narrative evidence of wrongful executions. The dependent variable is a multi-item scale measuring death penalty support. We will use moderated regression analysis (OLS with interaction terms) to test subgroup effects, with key moderators being a validated retributivism scale, political party identification, and racial resentment. Analyses will control for demographics and prior death penalty attitudes, using bootstrapping for robust standard errors.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a nuanced theory of 'punitive defensiveness' to the framed-group-position framework, demonstrating how justice-related information can produce backlash. It will provide empirical evidence challenging the linear model of informational persuasion in criminal justice attitudes and offer policymakers insight into the entrenched nature of death penalty support.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the assumed directional effect of innocence information on policy attitudes. It integrates Cullen's work on partisan-racial gradients with psychological theories of defensive processing to predict a counterintuitive 'backfire effect' specific to criminal justice.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Racial-Partisan Gradient: A Multivariate Test of the Stability of Racial Differences in Policing Attitudes
proposals.motivation: Criminological research, including Cullen's recent work, consistently identifies a significant racial gap in attitudes toward police and criminal justice policy, with African Americans expressing greater skepticism. However, pre-2026 critiques highlight a measurement crisis and the potential for omitted variable bias. It is crucial to determine if this racial difference is a stable, independent predictor or a statistical artifact of correlated factors like partisanship, ideology, and socioeconomic status, which are themselves racially patterned.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed effect of racial identity (Black vs. White) on policing attitudes will be substantially attenuated, and may not remain statistically significant, when simultaneously controlling for a robust set of political (partisanship, ideology), demographic (age, gender, education, income), and contextual (neighborhood crime, personal victimization) variables.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from the 2020 or 2024 American National Election Studies (ANES) Time Series, which includes validated measures of racial identity, party identification, political ideology, and attitudes toward police and criminal justice. Using hierarchical linear regression, we will first model attitudes as a function of race alone, then incrementally add blocks of control variables. The analysis will employ OLS regression for continuous outcomes and logistic regression for dichotomous items, with robust standard errors. Key diagnostics will include variance inflation factors to check for multicollinearity and comparisons of model fit (R-squared change) to assess the unique explanatory power of race.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous test of the robustness of a foundational finding in public opinion criminology. It will clarify whether 'race' operates as a master status in shaping views or is better understood through a framed-group-position lens where political and social identities are intertwined. The results will inform theories of attitude formation and have practical implications for how policymakers and police interpret public trust across communities.
proposals.novelty: While racial differences are well-documented, systematically testing their stability against the powerful confound of political partisanship—a factor shown in Cullen's recent work to be deeply racialized—is a critical next step. This moves beyond bivariate descriptions to a multivariate causal framework that addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by isolating the unique effect of racial group membership.

proposals.title: Beyond Fairness: Testing System Distrust and Moral Outrage as Mediators Between Innocence Beliefs and Support for Criminal Justice Reform
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, often implicitly assumes that perceptions of procedural fairness are the primary psychological pathway linking beliefs about systemic injustice (e.g., wrongful convictions) to public support for reform. This narrow focus may overlook more potent, affect-laden mechanisms, particularly in a pre-2026 political climate characterized by high polarization and declining institutional trust. Understanding these alternative mediators is crucial for crafting effective, resonant messages to build support for policies like universal second-look sentencing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between beliefs about the innocence of the convicted (especially along racial lines) and support for progressive criminal justice policies is more strongly mediated by (a) generalized distrust in the legal system and (b) feelings of moral outrage, than by cognitive assessments of procedural fairness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N~2,000) of U.S. adults. Using a factorial design, we will prime innocence beliefs with vignettes varying the race of a wrongfully convicted individual. We will measure the proposed mediators (fairness perceptions, system distrust, moral outrage) using validated multi-item scales. To test the mediation model, we will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation, comparing the indirect effects through each proposed mediator. We will control for key covariates identified in Cullen's prior work, including political ideology and racial resentment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more nuanced theoretical model of public opinion formation in criminal justice, moving beyond a fairness-centric framework. It will offer empirical evidence on whether emotional and trust-based reactions are more powerful drivers of policy support, which can inform advocacy and communication strategies. The findings will also contribute to the methodological literature by demonstrating a robust test of competing mediation pathways.
proposals.novelty: While fairness is a dominant construct in procedural justice literature, directly testing it against the understudied mechanisms of system-level distrust and moral outrage in the context of innocence beliefs is novel. This shifts the focus from how the system *should* operate to how people emotionally and evaluatively *react* to its perceived failures.

proposals.title: The Shifting Sands of Doubt: A Longitudinal and Framed-Group-Position Analysis of Belief in Executed Innocents, 2000-2025
proposals.motivation: Public belief that innocent people have been executed is a critical driver of declining support for capital punishment in the United States. Understanding the dynamics of this belief is essential for criminological theory and policy, yet prior research has been largely cross-sectional, failing to capture how this sentiment evolves alongside national discourse on racial injustice, high-profile exonerations, and partisan polarization. This study directly addresses the 'hidden measurement crisis' by systematically tracking this key attitudinal construct over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that belief in executed innocents has increased significantly since 2000, but that this trend is not uniform; it follows a racial-partisan gradient, with the steepest increases among Black Americans and White liberals, and the shallowest among White conservatives, reflecting a framed-group-position dynamic where exposure to racial injustice frames differentially shapes perceptions of systemic fallibility.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary longitudinal analysis of nationally representative survey data (e.g., Gallup, GSS, ANES) from 2000-2025, identifying consistent items measuring belief in executed innocents. Using hierarchical age-period-cohort (HAPC) modeling, we will decompose temporal trends. We will then integrate this with data on media framing (e.g., LexisNexis analysis of 'innocence' and 'race' in death penalty coverage) and key policy events (e.g., moratorium declarations, exoneration announcements) to test framed-group-position theory via time-series regression with subgroup interactions (Black, White Liberal, White Conservative).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive longitudinal map of a pivotal death penalty attitude, offering empirical clarity to theoretical debates about declining legitimacy. It will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory from policing to the ultimate state sanction, demonstrating how racial and partisan identities interact with public discourse to shape perceptions of systemic error. The findings will inform policy debates on sentencing reform and the future of capital punishment.
proposals.novelty: While belief in executed innocents is known to be prevalent, its longitudinal trajectory and its differential growth across racial-partisan subgroups—as predicted by a framed-group-position mechanism—are unexamined. This moves beyond documenting static attitudes to modeling dynamic attitudinal change as a political and racialized process.

proposals.title: Framing Innocence: A Framed-Group-Position Analysis of Media Coverage and Public Belief in Wrongful Executions
proposals.motivation: Public opinion on capital punishment is a critical driver of policy, with belief in the execution of innocent persons being a key predictor of opposition. Media coverage of exonerations is a primary channel through which the public encounters this issue, yet its specific role in shaping these innocence beliefs remains underexamined. This gap is significant given the documented racial-partisan polarization in criminal justice attitudes and the potential for media frames to activate group identities.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to media coverage of exonerations that frames the issue in terms of systemic racial bias will increase belief in the execution of innocent persons, particularly among racial and partisan groups whose position is primed by the frame, compared to coverage framed around procedural error or individual tragedy.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method design. First, a computational content analysis (using Latent Dirichlet Allocation topic modeling and dictionary-based sentiment analysis) of a large corpus of news articles (2010-2025) on exonerations and capital punishment will identify dominant media frames. Second, a nationally representative survey experiment will expose participants to randomly assigned news vignettes reflecting these frames. The dependent variable will be belief in executed innocents, with key moderators being racial identity and partisanship, analyzed using moderated regression models.
proposals.contribution: This research will directly extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by applying it to the understudied domain of wrongful executions, linking macro-level media discourse to micro-level attitudinal formation. It will provide empirical evidence on how media acts as a mechanism in the racial-partisan gradient of criminal justice attitudes, offering practical insights for advocates and policymakers on communication strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines media effects on death penalty support generally, this proposal uniquely isolates the 'innocence' dimension and tests how racialized versus non-racialized media frames interact with pre-existing group identities to shape this specific, potent belief.

proposals.title: Framing Innocence: An Experimental Test of Narratives on Racial-Partisan Divides in Death Penalty Support
proposals.motivation: Despite declining support, the death penalty remains a salient policy issue marked by profound racial and partisan cleavages, as highlighted by Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient. Pre-2026 research shows that innocence concerns are a primary driver of opposition, but how different narratives about wrongful convictions are framed and received by the public is not well understood. This gap limits the ability to explain persistent attitudinal divides and to communicate effectively about sentencing reform.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing innocence narratives around systemic racial bias in wrongful convictions will polarize attitudes along racial and partisan lines, increasing opposition among Black and Democratic respondents but decreasing it among White Republican respondents. Conversely, a race-neutral 'human error' framing will produce a smaller, less polarized reduction in support.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative, online survey experiment (N≈2,400) with a 3x2 between-subjects factorial design. Participants will be randomly assigned to read a short vignette about a capital case. The primary manipulation will vary the narrative framing of innocence: (1) Control (no innocence information), (2) 'Human Error' frame (focused on eyewitness misidentification), or (3) 'Systemic Racial Bias' frame (linking wrongful conviction to racial disparities in policing and prosecution). A secondary manipulation will vary the defendant's race (Black/White). We will measure death penalty support post-exposure using established scales. Data will be analyzed using OLS regression with interaction terms to test for differential effects by respondent race, partisanship, and their intersection, controlling for key covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by applying it to the death penalty context, specifying how innocence frames activate group identities and political allegiances. It will provide causal evidence on how communication strategies may inadvertently widen or narrow attitudinal divides on criminal justice policy. The findings will offer practical insights for advocates and policymakers on the consequential framing of reform arguments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes the importance of innocence, this study is novel in experimentally dissecting how *different types* of innocence narratives function as political frames, explicitly testing their capacity to exacerbate or mitigate the well-documented racial-partisan gradient in punitive attitudes.

proposals.title: The Networked Structure of Belief: Mapping the Connections Between Belief in Executed Innocents and Criminal Justice Attitudes
proposals.motivation: Public opinion is central to democratic legitimacy in criminal justice policy. Belief in executed innocents is a potent, morally charged attitude that may serve as a central node in a network of related justice beliefs, influencing support for policies like the death penalty, police reform, and sentencing review. Understanding its structural connections, particularly across racial and partisan lines, is crucial for explaining polarization and identifying leverage points for policy communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that belief in executed innocents is a central, highly connected node in a network of criminal justice attitudes, and that the strength and pattern of its connections will vary systematically by race and political ideology, as predicted by framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from a new, nationally representative survey measuring belief in executed innocents alongside a battery of established criminal justice attitude items (e.g., support for death penalty, police legitimacy, racial bias in system, punitive sentiment). Using R and the `qgraph` package, we will estimate a Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) to map the partial correlation network of these attitudes. We will calculate node centrality metrics (strength, betweenness) and conduct network comparison tests to analyze structural differences between Black/White and Democrat/Republican subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond simple bivariate correlations to model the systemic architecture of criminal justice opinions, revealing how core beliefs are interrelated. It will empirically test and extend framed-group-position theory by visualizing how group identities shape entire belief systems. The findings will offer a novel, structural explanation for attitude stability and resistance to change.
proposals.novelty: While belief in executed innocents has been studied as a predictor, its role as a potential hub in an attitude network is unexplored. Applying network analysis to this question represents a significant methodological innovation for public opinion research in criminology, moving from variable-centered to system-centered models.

proposals.title: The Policy Gap: Testing the Link Between Public Support and Legislative Action for Incarcerated Women in Restrictive States
proposals.motivation: Criminological research, including Cullen's work on 'universal second look' sentencing, consistently documents public support for progressive reforms, yet punitive policies persist, especially in conservative states. This gap is acute for incarcerated women, a population facing unique vulnerabilities but often overlooked in policy debates. The disconnect between public opinion and policy outcomes challenges core democratic and criminological theories about the drivers of legal change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, controlling for state-level political and demographic factors, majority public support for reforms targeting incarcerated women will have a significantly weaker association with actual policy adoption in states with restrictive political cultures compared to more liberal states.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, longitudinal state-level analysis. First, we will measure state-level public opinion using aggregated national survey data (e.g., GSS, ANES) and apply multilevel regression and poststratification (MRP) to estimate support for key reforms (e.g., expanded visitation, prenatal care, alternative sentencing) for incarcerated women. Second, we will create a legislative outcome database tracking the introduction and passage of relevant bills from 2010-2025. Finally, we will use time-series cross-sectional (TSCS) regression models with fixed effects to test the relationship between lagged opinion estimates and policy adoption, interacting opinion with a validated measure of state political culture (e.g., Berry et al. index).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct empirical test of the 'public opinion drives policy' axiom in a critical, understudied domain. It will advance framed-group-position theory by examining how the political and racial context moderates the translation of support into law. The findings will inform advocacy strategy by identifying the specific conditions under which public sentiment is most likely to be effective.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes opinion-policy gaps, this study uniquely focuses on incarcerated women and explicitly tests how state political context conditions this relationship, moving beyond documenting support to explaining the mechanisms of (in)action. It integrates Cullen's recent focus on racial-partisan gradients in policing attitudes into the domain of correctional policy.

proposals.title: The Shield of Solidarity: Identifying Contexts and Populations with Default Opposition to Police Monitoring and Oversight
proposals.motivation: Research on public attitudes toward police oversight has predominantly focused on explaining support for reforms, often finding majority backing for policies like body-worn cameras. This focus overlooks the significant minority who consistently oppose such measures. Understanding the contexts and characteristics of this opposition is crucial for a complete theoretical account of police legitimacy and for designing oversight mechanisms that are broadly perceived as legitimate, not just by reformers but by skeptical constituencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that opposition to police oversight policies is not random but is concentrated in specific populations (e.g., politically conservative, high police identification, majority-white communities with low crime) and is most pronounced when oversight is framed as punitive, externally imposed, or threatening to officer autonomy and group status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N=~2,500) of U.S. adults. The design will use a factorial framework to test the effects of oversight policy type (e.g., body cameras, civilian review boards, federal consent decrees) and message framing (procedural justice vs. punitive accountability vs. officer safety) on support. We will employ hierarchical regression and latent class analysis to identify distinct attitudinal typologies. The analysis will specifically model interaction effects between policy frames and respondent characteristics (party ID, racial identity, prior trust in police, local crime rates) to pinpoint the conditions where opposition is the default response.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic map of opposition to police oversight, moving beyond demographic correlates to explain the conditional and framed nature of this resistance. It will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by applying it to the 'negative case' of opposition, and offer evidence-based guidance on how to communicate oversight reforms to reduce polarization and build wider coalitions for change.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant question in the literature from 'Who supports oversight and why?' to 'Who opposes it and under what conditions?', a critical but neglected perspective. It integrates political psychology's insights on partisan motivated reasoning with criminological theories of legitimacy to explain opposition.

proposals.title: Framing Police Misconduct: A Framed-Group-Position Experiment on Victim-Blaming and Backfire Effects in Public Support for Reform
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 context of intense public debate over policing and racial justice, reformers often assume that publicizing misconduct will bolster support for reform. However, existing research on framed-group-position theory and racial-partisan gradients suggests that certain framings may activate partisan or racialized schemas, potentially backfiring. This study directly tests this critical, under-examined risk to ensure advocacy strategies are empirically sound and do not inadvertently undermine their own goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framings of police misconduct which emphasize victim behavior or criminal history will trigger victim-blaming attributions, particularly among individuals with high racial resentment or conservative political identities, thereby reducing their support for structural police reform policies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, pre-registered, factorial survey experiment with a nationally representative sample of American adults (N~3,000). Using a 2x2x2 between-subjects design, we will manipulate vignettes describing an incident of police misconduct along key framing dimensions: the race of the civilian (Black/White), the civilian's prior record (mentioned/not mentioned), and the nature of the civilian's non-compliance (passive/verbally confrontational). Primary outcomes will be support for specific reform policies (e.g., universal second look, civilian review boards). We will use moderated mediation analysis (PROCESS Model 7) to test if the interaction of framing and respondent partisanship/racial resentment influences support via the mediator of victim-blaming attributions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental test of the 'backfire hypothesis' in police reform messaging, moving beyond assumptions about information effects. It will extend framed-group-position theory by specifying the causal mechanisms—victim-blaming attributions—through which racial-partisan gradients in attitudes are activated. The findings will offer concrete, evidence-based guidance for advocates and policymakers on how to communicate about misconduct effectively.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents partisan and racial divides in policing attitudes, it is non-obvious that providing *more* information about misconduct could *reduce* support for reform. This proposal uniquely posits a causal, psychological backfire effect rooted in victim-blaming, a mechanism not centrally examined in prior criminological framing studies.

proposals.title: The Hierarchy of Harms: Public Perceptions of Dehumanization, Procedural Injustice, and Physical Safety in Policing
proposals.motivation: Criminological and sociological literature often posits dehumanization as a primary harm of punitive systems, particularly in racially disparate policing. However, public opinion research, especially Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, suggests public attitudes are complexly framed by group position and policy salience. It is unclear whether the public ranks dehumanization as the most salient harm compared to more tangible concerns like physical safety or procedural fairness, a gap with critical implications for building public support for reform.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, for the American public, perceived harms related to physical safety and procedural injustice will be more salient drivers of attitudes toward policing than abstract concerns about dehumanization, and that this hierarchy of harms will vary significantly across racial and partisan groups as predicted by framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N~2,400) using a conjoint design. Respondents will evaluate vignettes describing police encounters where the levels of three harm dimensions (dehumanizing language/treatment, procedural violations, physical safety threats) are randomly manipulated. We will measure perceived wrongfulness, support for officer discipline, and policy attitudes. Analysis will employ hierarchical Bayesian logistic regression and average marginal component effects (AMCEs) to estimate the relative causal weight of each harm dimension, with interactions for respondent race, partisanship, and prior police exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will empirically test a core theoretical assumption about the nature of policing harms, directly contributing to the literature on public opinion and legitimacy. It will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by identifying the specific harm dimensions that activate racial and partisan cleavages, offering evidence-based guidance for advocates and policymakers on how to effectively frame reform messages to diverse publics.
proposals.novelty: While dehumanization is a central concept in critical theory, its relative empirical salience in public opinion has not been rigorously tested against competing harms. This study moves beyond documenting attitudes to causally identifying the hierarchy of harms that shape them, a novel application of experimental methods to a core theoretical debate.

proposals.title: The Severity-Support Nexus: How Public Support for Police Reform Varies by Type and Severity of Misconduct
proposals.motivation: Public opinion is crucial for legitimizing and implementing police reforms. While research has examined general attitudes toward police, there is a significant gap in understanding how support for specific, concrete policy interventions varies across distinct types of officer misconduct (e.g., excessive force vs. verbal degradation). This granularity is essential for designing targeted, publicly-supported accountability mechanisms in the pre-2026 context of intense national debate over policing and racial justice.
proposals.hypothesis: Public support for specific police accountability policies (e.g., mandatory suspension, decertification, civil liability) will be significantly higher for misconduct involving physical abuse or racial animus than for verbal degradation or procedural violations, and this relationship will be moderated by racial and partisan identity as predicted by framed-group-position theory.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N~2,500) using a factorial vignette design. Respondents will be randomly assigned to read a brief scenario describing a police misconduct incident, with factors manipulated across vignettes: misconduct type (physical, verbal/profane, racial slur, procedural), severity, and officer/recipient demographics. Following the vignette, respondents will rate their support for a battery of policy consequences. Data analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to account for the nested factorial design and use interaction terms to test for moderation by racial and partisan identity, controlling for relevant covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, experimental mapping of public policy preferences onto a taxonomy of police misconduct. It will extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by applying it to specific policy outcomes rather than general attitudes. The findings will offer actionable evidence for policymakers and advocates on which reforms have the broadest public mandate for different categories of wrongdoing.
proposals.novelty: Existing work often treats 'police misconduct' as monolithic or focuses on broad confidence measures. This study's novelty lies in its disaggregation of misconduct into specific, behaviorally-defined types and its direct linkage to support for discrete policy remedies, moving beyond abstract attitudes to concrete policy support.

proposals.title: The Durability of Persuasion: A Longitudinal Panel Study on the Persistence of Information Effects Regarding Police Misconduct on Public Support for Reform
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient, demonstrates that information about police misconduct can shift public attitudes toward policing policy. However, these studies are almost exclusively cross-sectional, leaving a critical gap: we do not know if these attitude changes are ephemeral or durable. Understanding the temporal dynamics of persuasion is essential for assessing the potential of informational campaigns to foster lasting public support for evidence-based reforms, a key concern in the post-Ferguson, pre-2026 policy landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: The persuasive effect of information about police misconduct on support for reformist policies (e.g., universal second look sentencing, civilian oversight) will decay significantly over a six-month period, with the rate of decay moderated by individuals' pre-existing racial attitudes and political partisanship.
proposals.method: We will implement a three-wave longitudinal survey experiment using a national probability panel (e.g., NORC's AmeriSpeak or the Understanding America Study). At Wave 1 (T0), respondents will be randomly assigned to a treatment condition viewing a validated vignette and statistics on police misconduct or a control condition. Policy support and framed-group-position measures will be collected. Follow-up surveys at one month (T1) and six months (T2) will re-measure the same dependent variables without re-administering the treatment. Analysis will employ latent growth curve models and cross-lagged panel models to test for decay and moderation, using established scales for racial resentment and partisan identification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence on the durability of information effects in policing attitudes, directly addressing the 'hidden measurement crisis' related to temporal validity. It will advance framed-group-position theory by testing how group identities stabilize or destabilize attitude change over time, offering practical insights for advocates and policymakers on the necessary frequency of public engagement.
proposals.novelty: While cross-sectional experiments on information effects are common, longitudinal designs tracking the decay of persuasion in criminological policy attitudes are virtually non-existent. Further, integrating this temporal analysis with the moderating role of the racial-partisan gradient represents a novel theoretical synthesis.

proposals.title: Framing the Badge: An Experimental Test of Media Narratives on Public Support for Police Accountability Reforms
proposals.motivation: Public opinion is a critical driver of criminal justice policy, yet support for police reform remains polarized along racial and partisan lines. Media coverage of police misconduct is a primary source of information shaping these attitudes, but the specific narrative frames that can effectively increase support for concrete policy reforms among diverse publics are not well understood. This gap is significant for translating public concern into actionable policy change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that media frames emphasizing systemic accountability and procedural justice will be more effective at increasing support for police reform policies than frames emphasizing individual officer deviance, and that these effects will be moderated by racial identity and political partisanship, consistent with a framed-group-position model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, pre-registered online survey experiment with a nationally representative sample of U.S. adults (N~3,000). Participants will be randomly assigned to read a professionally crafted news article about a police misconduct incident, varying the narrative frame (e.g., 'bad apple' vs. 'rotten barrel' vs. 'procedural failure'). Following exposure, we will measure support for specific reform policies (e.g., universal second look sentencing, civilian review boards). We will analyze the data using factorial ANOVA and moderated regression models to test main and interactive effects of frame, race, and partisanship.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly extend Cullen's work on the racial-partisan gradient by isolating the causal impact of media framing. It will provide empirical evidence on which communication strategies are most effective for building a broad coalition for police accountability. The findings will offer practical guidance to advocates, policymakers, and journalists seeking to bridge partisan and racial divides on criminal justice reform.
proposals.novelty: While framing effects are well-studied, this project uniquely integrates them with Cullen's framed-group-position theory to test how narratives interact with pre-existing social identities to shape support for *specific, under-studied policies* like universal second look sentencing, moving beyond general attitudes toward police.

proposals.title: Networked Attitudes: Mapping the Diffusion of Prison Reform Sentiment Through Social and Partisan Affinity Networks
proposals.motivation: Public opinion is critical for criminal justice reform, yet we lack a dynamic understanding of how support for policies like second-look sentencing diffuses. Pre-2026 research, including Cullen's work, shows attitudes are shaped by racial and partisan identities, but the social network mechanisms—how attitudes spread through online/offline communities—remain a black box. This gap limits our ability to predict policy support shifts and design effective advocacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that attitudes toward prison reform diffuse through social networks not randomly, but along pathways defined by racial-group affinity and partisan homophily, creating distinct, polarized clusters of opinion that resist cross-cutting influence.
proposals.method: We will employ social network analysis (SNA) using data from Twitter (pre-2026 API) and the General Social Survey (GSS) network modules. Using a mixed-methods design, we will (1) construct follower/retweet networks from Twitter data related to reform hashtags, applying community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) to identify opinion clusters; (2) model diffusion using stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) in RSIena to test for homophily effects; and (3) integrate GSS data to validate offline network structures and attitudes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first network-based model of prison reform attitude diffusion, directly extending Cullen's framed-group-position theory into a structural, dynamic framework. It will offer criminologists and policymakers a map of opinion pathways, identifying key bridges and barriers to building broader public support for evidence-based reforms.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is common in sociology, its application to criminological attitude research is rare. This project innovatively merges SNA with Cullen's established work on racial-partisan gradients, moving from correlational findings to a test of diffusion mechanisms within the very social structures that shape group position.

proposals.title: The Shield and the Scale: A Framed-Group-Position Analysis of Public Support for Officer Protections Against Allegations
proposals.motivation: Research on policing attitudes has focused overwhelmingly on support for policies regulating officer misconduct, such as body cameras or civilian oversight. However, the dual policy domain—public support for measures protecting officers from false allegations (e.g., qualified immunity, stricter penalties for false reports)—remains empirically underexamined. Understanding this dual is critical in the pre-2026 context of intense political polarization and racialized debates over police legitimacy, as it reveals the competing moral and political frameworks the public applies to law enforcement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that support for officer protections is structured by a racial-partisan gradient, where White Republicans express the strongest support, and Black Democrats the weakest, but that this relationship is significantly moderated by how the policy is framed (e.g., as protecting officers' safety vs. preserving their discretion).
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey experiment (N≈2,400) using a 2x3 factorial design. The first factor will vary the racial identity of a vignette subject (Black/White citizen). The second will manipulate the policy frame (officer safety, procedural justice, or fiscal responsibility). Dependent variables will be Likert-scale measures of support for specific protections (e.g., presumption of truthfulness, legal indemnity). Analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling to test for main and interaction effects of partisanship, race, and frame, controlling for demographics and prior attitudes toward police.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, nationally representative map of support for officer protection policies, directly complementing the extensive literature on misconduct reforms. It will empirically test and extend Cullen's framed-group-position theory by applying it to this neglected policy domain. The findings will inform policymakers about the contested legitimacy of legal safeguards for police and the malleability of public opinion through framing.
proposals.novelty: While support for *regulating* police is well-studied, analyzing support for *protecting* police as its conceptual dual is a novel inversion. Applying a framed-group-position lens to this specific domain moves beyond documenting partisan divides to explain how racial identity and strategic framing interact to shape them.

proposals.title: Counter-Reform Narratives: Identifying Framing Effects that Reduce Public Support for Police Oversight and Accountability Policies
proposals.motivation: Research on criminal justice reform has focused on messages that build public support, but little is known about the counter-narratives that undermine it. Understanding these oppositional frames is critical for reform advocates to anticipate and counter resistance, and for scholars to develop a complete theory of public opinion formation on policing. This gap is especially salient in the pre-2026 context of intense partisan and racial polarization over police reform.
proposals.hypothesis: Drawing on framed-group-position theory, we hypothesize that narratives linking oversight policies to threats to public safety (e.g., increased crime) and to racialized partisan identity (e.g., as anti-police or favoring certain groups) will be most effective in decreasing support, particularly among white respondents and political conservatives.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of original, nationally representative survey experiments. Using a factorial design, we will test the independent and interactive effects of message frames (e.g., safety threat, fiscal cost, anti-police sentiment, racial resentment cues) on support for specific oversight policies (e.g., civilian review boards, mandatory de-escalation). Analyses will employ OLS regression with interaction terms to model heterogeneous effects by respondent race, partisanship, and prior attitudes, controlling for key demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, experimental evidence of the specific narratives that erode support for police oversight, moving beyond descriptive accounts of opposition. It will empirically test and extend framed-group-position theory by identifying the most potent negative frames. The findings will offer practical strategic insights for the reform movement and a more balanced scholarly understanding of the dynamics of public opinion on policing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Cullen and others has mapped the racial-partisan gradient in support, this project innovatively focuses on experimentally manipulating and identifying the *causal messaging drivers of decreased support*, the inverse of typical reform-focused research. It applies a theory of framed-group positions to explain opposition, not just alignment.

proposals.title: The Framed-Group-Position of Immigration Enforcement: Public Support for Officer Monitoring in ICE Detention Facilities
proposals.motivation: Public attitudes toward policing have been extensively studied, but support for oversight of officers in carceral contexts beyond traditional policing, such as Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) detention facilities, remains largely unexplored. Given the documented concerns about conditions and accountability in immigration detention pre-2026, understanding the public's stance on monitoring policies is crucial for democratic oversight and policy reform. This research scales Cullen's framed-group-position theory to a novel, high-stakes institutional setting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that support for officer monitoring in ICE facilities will be structured by a racial-partisan gradient, but that this relationship will be significantly moderated by how the policy is framed (e.g., as ensuring officer safety/professionalism vs. preventing abuse of detainees).
proposals.method: We will field a nationally representative survey of U.S. adults (n=~2,000) using an experimental vignette design. Respondents will be randomly assigned to different frames for a proposed body-worn camera and external review policy for ICE officers. Support will be the dependent variable. We will employ hierarchical regression and moderation analysis using the PROCESS macro in SPSS to test the interaction between respondent characteristics (race, partisanship) and experimental frame on policy support, controlling for demographics and prior attitudes.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the framed-group-position theory into the understudied domain of immigration control, testing its generalizability. It will provide the first systematic, experimental evidence on the drivers of public support for accountability within ICE, informing debates on detention oversight. The findings will contribute to the literature on public opinion, race, and carceral institutions beyond the criminal justice system.
proposals.novelty: While research on police monitoring is established, applying this theoretical and empirical lens to ICE officers is novel, as it involves a distinct legal authority (administrative vs. criminal law) and a different public narrative. The explicit test of whether frames can mitigate or exacerbate racial-partisan divides in this specific context is a new contribution.

ideas.title: Framing Justice: Media Narratives and the Procedural Validation of Causal Attributions
ideas.description: This research experimentally tests how different media narratives (e.g., excusatory vs. individual-responsibility frames) shape public endorsement of causal attributions for crime, while simultaneously using a procedural justice scale to adjudicate how those same narratives affect the perceived legitimacy and validity of the criminological measures presented within the stories. The design exposes participants to news articles that vary the narrative frame but present identical underlying data from established criminological scales, allowing researchers to disentangle the effect of the narrative from the effect of the measure. The synergy lies in evaluating whether media framing can bias perceptions of procedural justice in scientific reporting, thereby validating or undermining trust in social science metrics based on narrative context rather than methodological rigor.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunization Theory from Public Health to Reduce Ideological Polarization on Policing
ideas.description: This idea applies the psychological principle of cognitive dissonance through the analogous framework of immunization theory from public health. The analogous solution from epidemiology involves preemptively inoculating individuals against misinformation by exposing them to a weakened form of a myth alongside a refutation, building cognitive resistance. Adapted to the research on ideological polarization regarding policing, the method would involve a cognitive dissonance paradigm where participants are first exposed to a weakened, easily critiqued version of an argument from the ideological opposite (e.g., a poorly reasoned 'defund the police' or 'unqualified police support' statement). By guiding participants to actively counter-argue this weak version, the intervention aims to create cognitive antibodies, inducing a form of dissonance that reinforces their critical faculties rather than their partisan identity. This 'attitudinal immunization' is predicted to reduce susceptibility to future polarizing messages, thereby decreasing the intensity of ideological polarization on the issue over time.

ideas.title: A Longitudinal Dissonance & Inoculation System for Sustainable Attitude Change on Policing
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond a single experiment to test a multi-component intervention designed for lasting impact. It first uses a cognitive dissonance task to prompt a shift in attributions for a policing incident. This is immediately followed by a series of sessions that inoculate participants against social pushback by having them analyze and refute common counter-messages from their own ideological side. The protocol includes periodic values-affirmation exercises to maintain openness and requires small, escalating public commitments to the new perspective. The design measures attitude stability, resistance to persuasive counter-attacks, and behavioral intentions over several months to assess durability.

ideas.title: Comparative Institutional Analysis of Police Reform in Matched Cities With and Without Recent Scandal
ideas.description: This research employs a comparative case study design to move beyond a single outlier. Researchers will select 3-4 carefully matched cities based on size, demographic composition, and political structure, creating a sample that includes both cities with recent high-profile misconduct scandals and comparable cities without such a recent catalyzing event. The study will then replicate core measurements—such as policy changes, oversight activity, and officer surveys—across all sites simultaneously. This design allows for systematic comparison to distinguish between reforms driven specifically by scandal from those arising from broader secular trends, producing findings with greater external validity and identifying actionable institutional pathways.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of attribution change after events using ecological momentary assessment and network analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from epidemiology to track how public attributions for police misconduct shift after high-profile incidents or reform implementations. The analogous solution from public health involves using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and social network analysis to study the real-time diffusion of behaviors or beliefs. The adapted idea is to conduct a longitudinal study where a diverse participant panel uses a smartphone app to provide frequent, in-the-moment reports on their causal attributions regarding police conduct. This high-frequency temporal data will be enriched with information about each participant's information sources and social networks. By applying network diffusion models, the research will identify key nodes, pathways, and the velocity of attribution change, providing a dynamic, mechanistic understanding of how public opinion crystallizes or shifts in response to specific events and policy interventions over time.

ideas.title: Elite Discourse as a Causal Driver: Testing the Priming and Framing of Public Attributions
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that elite discourse merely reflects public opinion by testing its causal role in shaping public attributions. Drawing an analogy from epidemiology's methods for establishing causality, the study adapts longitudinal cohort designs and natural experiments. It would track a representative public panel over time, measuring their exposure to specific elite frames (via media diet analysis) and their attributions for political outcomes, using events like economic shifts or foreign policy crises as intervention points. By analyzing temporal sequences and controlling for confounders like partisanship, the research can isolate whether elite discourse drives changes in public attributions, moving beyond correlational evidence.

ideas.title: Adaptive Cognitive Dissonance Interventions for Dynamic Polarization Measurement
ideas.description: This research synthesizes cognitive dissonance paradigms with adaptive measurement to study ideological polarization on policing. Instead of using a universal scale to measure attitude change before and after a dissonance intervention, it employs context-sensitive, evolving measures that track an individual's attributional shifts in real-time during the experiment. This allows the dissonance task (e.g., the counter-attitudinal essay prompt or feedback) to be dynamically adjusted in intensity or content based on the participant's current measured state of dissonance and resistance. Consequently, the research can model the nonlinear process of polarization reduction, identifying personalized pathways and critical inflection points where interventions are most effective, rather than just assessing a static outcome.

ideas.title: Adapting Sensory Analysis Conjoint Designs to Measure Public Judgment of Misconduct
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodological solution from consumer sensory science, where choice-based conjoint analysis is used to deconstruct preferences for multi-attribute products, to the problem of measuring public judgment of misconduct. In the analogous field, researchers present participants with systematically varied product bundles (e.g., differing in sweetness, texture) to isolate the part-worth utility of each attribute on overall liking. Adapted to political science, a conjoint experiment would present respondents with pairs of misconduct vignettes where attributes (cause, intent, partisanship, outcome) are randomly varied. By analyzing choices about which scenario is more severe, we can causally identify the weight the public assigns to each specific misconduct cause or characteristic, providing a granular, quantitative map of the drivers of public judgment.

ideas.title: Deep Contextual Interrogation: Using a High-Profile Case to Test Competing Theoretical Frameworks
ideas.description: This study conducts an intensive, multi-method replication within a single city that has experienced a recent, high-profile misconduct incident. The core innovation is to treat this bounded case as a living laboratory to directly test whether inconsistent findings in the broader literature stem primarily from measurement problems or from deeper theoretical ambiguities. Researchers will apply multiple competing theoretical frameworks (e.g., organizational culture, institutional anomie, procedural justice) and their associated operationalizations to the same local data, including surveys, administrative records, and qualitative interviews. By holding the empirical context constant, the design isolates whether divergent results are an artifact of measurement choices or if they reveal genuinely incompatible theoretical explanations, thereby clarifying the fundamental crisis in the field.

ideas.title: Dynamic Meta-Validation: Using an Adaptive Procedural Justice Scale to Contextually Adjudicate Existing Measures
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive, context-specific scale of procedural justice whose parameters and item weights evolve based on situational factors like community trust levels or institutional type. This dynamic scale is then used as the benchmark to adjudicate the validity of existing static criminological measures, not to declare them universally valid or invalid, but to map the specific contexts in which they hold or fail. The synergy produces a meta-validation methodology that moves beyond one-time comparisons to generate a living, evidence-based map of measurement ecology, showing how the utility of established tools depends on the adaptive fit of the underlying construct they aim to capture.

ideas.title: Mapping the Incentive-Semantic Gap in Criminological Measurement
ideas.description: This research program uses computational linguistics (NLP) to systematically analyze the semantic content, redundancy, and construct validity of widely used criminology scales, not as a purely technical exercise, but to generate quantitative evidence of measurement stagnation or drift. It then correlates these linguistic and psychometric features with the institutional and career contexts (e.g., journal preferences, dominant theories, training pipelines) in which the scales were developed and continue to be used. The synergy produces a diagnostic map showing precisely how specific incentive structures lead to the persistence of known poor measures, moving beyond anecdotal awareness to a data-driven model of the field's measurement economy.

ideas.title: Mapping the Elite Discourse Network: How Elite-Driven Attributions Structure Public Belief Systems
ideas.description: This research maps the network of public beliefs (attributions, racial attitudes, ideology) to identify its central nodes, but crucially treats this network structure as a dependent variable shaped by elite political discourse. It investigates how elite-driven attributions—especially those about inequality—do not merely reflect but actively reconfigure the public's belief network, altering which nodes become central and how beliefs are linked. The synergy allows for testing which specific elite frames are most potent in restructuring the entire belief system, revealing the mechanisms of top-down ideological change.

ideas.title: Theory-First, Adversarially-Aligned Psychometric Development for Core Constructs
ideas.description: This approach systematically develops measures for constructs like legitimacy or deterrence by first rigorously defining their theoretical structure through adversarial debate and modeling. Experts and critics collaboratively map the construct's dimensions, boundaries, and hypothesized relationships before any psychometric work begins. The subsequent measurement development process is then explicitly designed to test and validate this a priori theoretical model, ensuring the final instrument is both psychometrically robust and deeply grounded in the construct's unique substantive theory. This creates measures that are defensible against criticisms of being mere methodological artifacts or poor conceptual fits.

ideas.title: Applying Attributional Ecology to Authority Misconduct
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Attributional Ecology in invasive species research to scale the attribution framework for authority misconduct. In ecology, the impact of an invasive species is analyzed through a dual lens: the species' intrinsic traits and the invaded ecosystem's vulnerabilities (invasibility). Analogously, for misconduct by teachers, politicians, or military personnel, we propose a framework that attributes responsibility not solely to the individual's actions but to the interaction between those actions and the specific susceptibilities of the institutional, cultural, or regulatory 'ecosystem' that enabled or failed to prevent the misconduct. This creates a scalable, comparative model for assessing the primary locus of blame—personal agency versus systemic failure—across different types of authority figures and their contexts.

ideas.title: Priming Narrative Frames: A Signal Detection Approach to Attribution Formation
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological solution from signal detection theory in cognitive neuroscience to the problem of media framing effects on attribution formation. In perceptual tasks, SDT elegantly separates sensitivity from bias. The adapted experiment would expose participants to different media frames (e.g., excusatory vs. multifaceted narratives) about a socio-political event. Subsequently, participants would be presented with a series of factual causal statements about the event, each with a verifiable truth value (established by prior evidence presented to the participant). Their task is to endorse or reject each statement as a primary cause. By analyzing hits (endorsing true causes) and false alarms (endorsing false causes) across framing conditions, we can compute a 'framing bias' metric (criterion) showing how much the narrative pushes the decision threshold, and an 'attributional sensitivity' metric (d') showing their ability to discern factual causes, independent of the frame. This provides a rigorous, quantitative measure of the pure biasing power of media narratives.

ideas.title: Develop a dynamic, equity-focused framework for criminological scale development and validation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes scale construction and validation into a unified, iterative framework. It develops formal methodological guidelines that mandate and detail prospective plans for testing measurement invariance across key demographic and geographic groups during the initial construction phase. The framework treats invariance testing not as a final validation step, but as critical diagnostic feedback used to iteratively refine item pools, response formats, and administration protocols. The ultimate output is a set of equity-informed standards and a companion toolkit that enables criminologists to build scales that are psychometrically sound and conceptually equivalent across populations from the outset.

ideas.title: Dynamic Conjoint Panel for Validating Causal Weights in Real-World Judgment Updating
ideas.description: This research integrates a traditional conjoint experiment into a multi-wave longitudinal panel to overcome its artificiality. It first uses the conjoint design to establish the baseline causal weight of different misconduct attributes in a controlled setting. Then, it tracks the same participants over time, measuring how their judgments update in response to actual, unfolding news events involving misconduct, testing whether the conjoint-derived weights predict real-world opinion change. This hybrid design validates the external relevance of the isolated causal parameters while capturing the dynamic and contextual nature of public judgment formation.

ideas.title: A Theory-Driven, Causal-Inference Framework for Validating Scale Structure Using Computational Linguistics and Psychometrics
ideas.description: This research system employs computational linguistics (NLP) not merely for descriptive semantic analysis, but to generate formal, testable hypotheses about construct overlap and discriminant validity in criminological scales. These hypotheses are grounded in both the semantic analysis and substantive criminological theory. The system then rigorously tests these hypotheses by applying advanced confirmatory psychometric models (e.g., structural equation modeling, item response theory) to large-scale empirical response datasets. The final output is a validated, evidence-based recommendation for scale refinement, retention, or restructuring, where any change is supported by both statistical evidence and theoretical coherence.

ideas.title: Normative Stopping Rules for Multifaceted Causal Attribution
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simply advocating for multifaceted attributions by developing and testing practical frameworks for deciding when such an attribution is 'good enough.' It investigates how different normative goals (e.g., justice, policy efficacy, public understanding) imply different criteria for halting the expansion of causal factors. The project will create a typology of stopping rules and evaluate their application in case studies from climate change and public health, assessing their impact on accountability, decisional clarity, and perceived legitimacy.

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Institutional Mapping for Authority Misconduct Attribution
ideas.description: This research begins by analyzing the distinct institutional landscapes of teachers, politicians, and military personnel to identify how power is structured and accountability is enforced within each system. It then develops tailored attribution frameworks that integrate these domain-specific factors, such as union protections in education, electoral accountability in politics, and chain-of-command structures in the military. This allows for a valid, context-sensitive application of attribution theory to misconduct within each domain. Finally, a meta-framework identifies transferable principles across domains, enabling robust, institutionally-aware comparisons without forcing a one-size-fits-all model.

ideas.title: Modeling Attributional Dynamics: State Variability, Predictable Patterns, and Ideological Boundary Conditions
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the simple state/trait dichotomy to model attributional thinking as a dynamic system with measurable parameters of variability and stability. It investigates how attributions fluctuate within individuals across time and contexts, identifying personal baselines and ranges of change. The design tests which situational features—such as perceived threat, social identity salience, or cognitive load—reliably drive attributional shifts, and whether these patterns of malleability are themselves consistent within individuals, forming a 'meta-trait'. Crucially, it examines how ideological dispositions moderate these dynamics, specifying the boundary conditions of attributional change.

ideas.title: Cross-Cultural Calibration of Procedural Justice Metrics Using Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Ecology and Biogeography—specifically Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM)—to the problem of validating a procedural justice scale across diverse populations. In ENM, scientists build a model of a species' habitat requirements in one region and test its transferability to new, distinct regions to assess generalizability and identify region-specific limitations. Analogously, this study would treat different demographic (e.g., cultural, socioeconomic) and geographic groups as distinct 'ecological niches' for the construct of procedural justice. The adapted methodology involves a multi-stage process: first, the scale is calibrated and its factor structure established in a primary reference population. Then, it is rigorously tested for measurement invariance across a series of novel, pre-defined 'niche' populations. The analysis would go beyond standard invariance testing to identify which specific items or sub-dimensions of the scale are 'non-transferable' (i.e., function differently) in specific contexts, much like how ENM identifies environmental variables that limit a species' distribution in new areas. This approach provides a structured, diagnostic framework for pinpointing the sources of measurement bias and informing the development of either a universally robust scale or a set of context-adapted calibrations.

ideas.title: Dynamic Contextualization for Responsible Multifaceted Attribution
ideas.description: This research investigates how to design adaptive, context-sensitive systems for multifaceted attribution that actively mitigate its potential downsides, such as policy paralysis or diffused accountability. Instead of applying a fixed, universal set of attribution weights and measures, the system would employ evolving, context-specific scales that prioritize different causal facets based on the decision stage, available evidence, and normative goals (e.g., urgency, accountability, learning). This dynamic contextualization allows decision-makers to navigate complexity without being overwhelmed, ensuring attributions remain both normatively rigorous and practically actionable. The core challenge becomes defining the rules and triggers for how the attribution framework itself should adapt to different governance and operational contexts.

ideas.title: Inductive Psychometric Development via Narrative Analysis
ideas.description: This research applies computational text analysis (NLP) to large corpora of open-ended narrative data (e.g., from interviews, social media, or public comments) to systematically identify the latent dimensions, semantic boundaries, and folk theories that constitute complex social science constructs like legitimacy or deterrence. The discovered linguistic patterns and attribution frameworks then directly inform and ground the creation of new, ecologically valid survey items and multi-dimensional scales. This synergy moves beyond validating existing scales with text or applying NLP to predefined constructs, instead using narrative data as the primary source for inductive psychometric theory-building.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Network Models to Map Belief Contagion and Resistance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from epidemiology, where network models trace pathogen spread to identify critical hubs for intervention, to the domain of social psychology. The analogous problem of containing a disease outbreak is solved by modeling contact networks, calculating centrality metrics to find super-spreaders, and targeting those nodes. Adapted to the research context, we map networks of belief connecting attributions, racial attitudes, and ideology, but treat beliefs as contagions. By applying epidemic diffusion models and network centrality algorithms to social-psychological data, we can identify the central, 'contagious' belief nodes. This allows for the identification of the most influential belief hubs, pinpointing optimal targets for interventions designed to disrupt or reshape the entire ideological network.

ideas.title: Mapping Theoretical Ambiguity onto Measurement Artifacts via Computational Linguistics
ideas.description: This research program uses NLP to analyze the semantic and syntactic structure of criminological scale items, but with the explicit goal of quantifying the degree of theoretical ambiguity present within and across measurement tools. By mapping linguistic features (e.g., keyword co-occurrence networks, frame analysis, conceptual density) to competing theoretical constructs, it can empirically demonstrate whether inconsistent findings stem from poor measurement of a clear construct or from scales inadvertently measuring different, theoretically muddled constructs. This approach moves beyond scale validation to provide evidence-based diagnostics for whether the field's crisis is primarily one of measurement engineering or requires prior theoretical clarification.

ideas.title: Convergent Validation of Cultural Models: Integrating Structured Elicitation, Behavioral Experiments, and Natural Language Analysis
ideas.description: This redesigned research system moves beyond subjective interviews to rigorously test the relationship between cultural models and attribution patterns. It first uses structured cognitive anthropological tasks (e.g., pile-sorts) to quantitatively map shared cultural schemas within a population. These elicited models are then used to predict attribution judgments in controlled, randomized behavioral experiments with standardized stimuli, establishing causal links. Finally, the prevalence and real-world expression of these models are validated through computational analysis of large-scale natural language corpora, ensuring findings are both robust and ecologically valid.

ideas.title: Causal Evidence for Measurement-Induced Theoretical Distortion: A Simulation and Instrumental Variable Framework
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond correlation to establish causal evidence that psychometrically weak scales distort theoretical conclusions. It uses large-scale simulation studies where the true data-generating process is known, explicitly modeling how noise, bias, and multidimensionality in scales alter effect sizes and structural relationships. The program then translates these models into testable instrumental variable designs for real-world literature, seeking instances where the choice or quality of a measurement instrument varies quasi-randomly to estimate the causal effect of poor measurement on reported findings.

ideas.title: Incentive-Compatible Metrics: Adapting Solutions from Clinical Trial Reform
ideas.description: This research inverts the assumption that scholars are unaware of poor measures by studying the institutional and career incentives that maintain reliance on them despite awareness. It draws an analogy from clinical medicine, which solved the analogous problem of publication bias and selective reporting through structural reforms like mandatory prospective trial registration and journal policy enforcement. Adapting this solution, the study proposes and examines the potential impact of a mandatory 'Methods and Metrics Registry' for social science, coupled with journal policies requiring pre-registration of measures. This institutional intervention aims to re-align incentives by rewarding transparent, pre-justified methodology over the continued use of known-but-expedient poor measures, making the choice of better metrics a career-advantageous strategy.

ideas.title: Dynamic Perturbation Analysis of Belief Networks: Identifying Context-Dependent and Causally Central Nodes
ideas.description: This research employs an experimental network perturbation framework to move beyond static correlation maps. Participants' belief networks (connecting attributions, racial attitudes, and ideology) are measured at multiple time points, interspersed with randomized exposures to contextual primes or challenging arguments. Using longitudinal network models, the analysis identifies which nodes show stable centrality versus which shift in response to perturbations, distinguishing structural cores from context-dependent connections. This allows for causal inference about influence pathways and identifies nodes whose centrality is robust across contexts versus measurement artifacts.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery of Crisis Sources: Disentangling Theoretical Ambiguity from Measurement Error in Meta-Analytic Networks
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond speculative debate by constructing a computational model to empirically test whether theoretical ambiguity or measurement problems are the primary driver of inconsistent scientific findings. It formalizes key concepts into variables within a causal network model, which is then fitted to a large corpus of meta-analytic data spanning multiple disciplines (e.g., social psychology, biomedicine, economics). The output is a domain-specific diagnostic that quantifies the probable contribution of each crisis source, providing an evidence-based guide for where to target reform efforts—theory refinement or measurement innovation.

ideas.title: Theoretical Ambiguity as the 'Dark Matter' of Inconsistent Findings
ideas.description: Explore if theoretical ambiguity, not measurement, is the root cause of inconsistent findings. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics/cosmology, where inconsistent galactic rotation data was solved not by perfecting measurements of visible matter but by postulating a new theoretical construct (dark matter), this research adapts that solution. It proposes that inconsistent empirical findings in social or behavioral sciences may stem from 'theoretical dark matter'—unobserved theoretical constructs, implicit boundary conditions, or ambiguous definitions. The adapted research idea involves systematically diagnosing and formalizing these theoretical ambiguities within a domain to test if doing so resolves empirical inconsistencies more effectively than the continual refinement of measurement tools, thereby challenging the assumption that measurement error is the primary crisis.

ideas.title: Apply Narrative Medicine's Illness Narrative Elicitation to Uncover Cultural Models in Attribution
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from the field of Narrative Medicine within Medical Humanities to the original research seed. The analogous problem in medicine is the need to understand a patient's subjective experience and cultural framework of illness, which is often obscured by standard clinical questioning. The solution is the use of structured, open-ended narrative elicitation techniques (e.g., 'Tell me the story of your illness') designed to draw out the full, culturally-embedded personal narrative. Adapted to this research context, the method involves conducting ethnographic interviews where participants are asked to narrate the story of a specific event or outcome (e.g., a success, a failure, a social incident) in full, from beginning to end. By analyzing these elicited narratives—their structure, metaphors, causal linkages, and agency assignments—rather than just direct answers to 'why' questions, researchers can systematically uncover the deep cultural models and schematic templates that implicitly guide attribution patterns, providing a richer, more contextually grounded understanding than standard attribution surveys or interviews allow.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Latent Social Constructs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology's 'ecological niche modeling' (ENM) to the problem of measuring latent social constructs. In ENN, a species' niche—a latent construct—is mapped by correlating known occurrence data with measurable environmental variables. Analogously, for a construct like 'social disorganization,' researchers would first identify clear empirical instances ('occurrences') in specific neighborhoods or communities. They would then systematically measure a comprehensive set of contextual variables (e.g., residential turnover, poverty concentration, ethnic heterogeneity, institutional density) for those and other locations. Using the ENM-inspired methodological pipeline—including variable selection, model fitting (e.g., using maximum entropy or regression techniques), and rigorous validation via spatial cross-validation—this approach would produce a validated, generalizable model. This model would not just measure the construct at known points but could predict its intensity and distribution across new social landscapes, offering a systematic, replicable, and spatially explicit psychometric tool for core sociological and criminological constructs.
